procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: average-risk screening, first colonoscopy in an asymptomatic 52-year-old woman. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg), tolerated well. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with confirmation of the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. A single 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed in its entirety by cold snare polypectomy; the specimen was retrieved and sent to pathology. The remainder of the examined colon was normal with good mucosal visualization. No masses, no ulceration, no active bleeding, and no significant diverticulosis. Retroflexion in the rectum was normal; no internal hemorrhoids of note. IMPRESSION: Single 5 mm ascending colon polyp removed by cold snare; otherwise normal screening colonoscopy to the cecum. Pathology pending.

contacts.clinical_signal: Trace spotting after cold-snare polypectomy of a small polyp; no ongoing bleeding, no clot, no hemodynamic symptoms
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; minor self-limited spotting can occur after a small cold-snare polypectomy. Advise return precautions: call or seek care for repeated passage of dark/red blood, clots, lightheadedness, or severe abdominal pain.
contacts.rationale: Dangerous-sounding-but-benign trap: a small amount of pink after a clean cold-snare polypectomy in a patient on no anticoagulants is expected; the correct disposition is reassurance with clear return precautions rather than escalation.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: average-risk screening (first colonoscopy, overdue). SEDATION: moderate (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 100 mcg). PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). ANTITHROMBOTICS: low-dose aspirin 81 mg continued per protocol; OTC ibuprofen held for 7 days pre-procedure; OTC fish oil is a supplement — no hold required. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with confirmation of the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. A single 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy; the specimen was retrieved and sent to pathology (Specimen A). A 3 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon was removed by cold forceps and retrieved (Specimen B). Scattered sigmoid diverticula were noted without stigmata of bleeding. No mass, no active bleeding, no worrisome lesion. Hemostasis was intact at both polypectomy sites. IMPRESSION: Two small polyps removed (ascending 5 mm, descending 3 mm); mild sigmoid diverticulosis. Pathology pending. Overall a good-quality screening exam with adequate prep.

procedure.name: Bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + Colonoscopy)
procedure.indication: Unexplained iron-deficiency anemia (ferritin 6, Hgb 9.8, MCV 74) with resolved menstrual source; daily aspirin and regular NSAID use; intermittent dark stools
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 4 mg, fentanyl 100 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 (2/3 right, 2/3 transverse, 3/3 left) — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: EGD: second portion of duodenum. Colonoscopy: cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve intubation)

procedure_report:
PROCEDURE: Bidirectional endoscopy (EGD followed by colonoscopy). INDICATION: unexplained iron-deficiency anemia with resolved menstrual source; daily low-dose aspirin and regular OTC ibuprofen; intermittent dark stools. Peri-procedure antiplatelet: patient advised to hold self-directed aspirin 81 mg and ibuprofen prior to procedure; NSAIDs held. SEDATION: moderate. 

EGD FINDINGS: The esophagus was normal with a Z-line at 39 cm and no evidence of esophagitis or varices. The stomach demonstrated patchy antral erythema with several small (2-4 mm) non-bleeding erosions consistent with NSAID/aspirin-related gastropathy. A single 8 mm clean-based ulcer (Forrest III, no stigmata of recent hemorrhage) was identified in the duodenal bulb; the ulcer base was clean without visible vessel or adherent clot and was photodocumented (duodenal bulb ulcers are not routinely biopsied, as malignancy is exceedingly rare at this site). Biopsies were taken from the gastric antrum and body for H. pylori (specimen B) and random biopsies were obtained from the second portion of the duodenum to evaluate for celiac disease as part of the iron-deficiency anemia workup (specimen C). The remainder of the duodenum to the second portion was normal. 

COLONOSCOPY FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum. A single 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy (specimen A, retrieved). Scattered sigmoid diverticula without stigmata of bleeding. No mass, no angioectasia, no active bleeding. Terminal ileum intubated and normal. 

IMPRESSION: (1) 8 mm clean-based duodenal bulb ulcer plus antral erosions — likely aspirin/NSAID-related; probable occult upper-GI blood-loss source explaining the iron-deficiency anemia. (2) Gastric biopsies for H. pylori and duodenal biopsies for celiac disease pending. (3) 5 mm ascending colon polyp removed. (4) Sigmoid diverticulosis. Pathology pending.

procedure.name: Colonoscopy
procedure.indication: Change in bowel habit with narrowed stool caliber, unintentional weight loss (~8 lb), positive fecal occult blood, and family history of colorectal cancer — expedited diagnostic colonoscopy for suspected colorectal neoplasm
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesia-administered
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 — adequate (proximal segments adequate; some solid residue distal to obstructing lesion)
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); scope passed through the narrowed segment with pediatric colonoscope

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with biopsy and tattoo. INDICATION: alarm features (narrowed stool caliber, ~8 lb unintentional weight loss, positive FOBT, hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, family history of colon cancer) — expedited evaluation for colorectal neoplasm. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. PREP: BBPS 7 — adequate. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced with some difficulty through a partially obstructing, circumferential, ulcerated and friable mass in the sigmoid colon, approximately 35 cm from the anal verge, spanning roughly 4-5 cm and narrowing the lumen. A pediatric colonoscope was used to traverse the stenotic segment; the scope was then advanced to the cecum, confirmed by identification of the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. Multiple cold-forceps biopsies (6 fragments) were taken from the mass and sent to pathology. India ink tattoo was placed just distal to the lesion to mark it for surgical localization. The mass was friable and contact-bled; no active spontaneous bleeding, and no intervention required for hemostasis. Scattered sigmoid diverticula were noted without stigmata of bleeding. The remainder of the examined colon was normal. IMPRESSION: Partially obstructing ulcerated sigmoid mass, highly suspicious for colorectal adenocarcinoma — biopsies pending; lesion tattooed for localization. Diverticulosis.

findings.location: sigmoid colon (~35 cm from anal verge)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: colorectal cancer screening in a patient with a first-degree relative (father) diagnosed with colon cancer at approximately age 68 — above average risk. SEDATION: moderate sedation, well tolerated. Given the documented history of postoperative nausea and vomiting, anti-emetic prophylaxis with ondansetron 4 mg IV and dexamethasone 4 mg IV was administered prior to sedation, and the patient recovered without nausea or vomiting. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. Two diminutive polyps were identified — a 4 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon and a 3 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon — both removed by cold snare polypectomy and retrieved for histology. The remainder of the examined colon and rectum were normal. No mass, no ulceration, and no active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Two diminutive polyps (ascending and descending colon) removed; otherwise normal colonoscopy to the cecum with terminal ileum intubation. Pathology pending. Surveillance interval to be finalized once histology returns, taking into account the family history of colorectal cancer.

Daily fish oil is a dietary supplement, not a therapeutic anticoagulant; no anticoagulation hold was required and the patient was confirmed to be on no prescription antiplatelet/anticoagulant agent.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with cold snare polypectomy and mass biopsy. INDICATION: 45-year-old man with ~2 weeks of hematochezia (bright red blood coating the stool), first-degree relative (father) with colorectal cancer diagnosed ~age 50, and regular OTC ibuprofen use; longstanding unconfirmed patient-reported hemorrhoids. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl). PREP: adequate, BBPS 8/9. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with intubation of the terminal ileum, which was normal. In the sigmoid colon there was a partially circumferential, firm, ulcerated mass approximately 3.5 cm with friability and contact bleeding, mildly narrowing the lumen but traversable with the adult scope; the lesion was not resected — multiple cold-forceps biopsies were obtained (specimen A) and the site was tattooed distally with submucosal India ink for future localization. A 7 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy and retrieved (specimen B). A 4 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy and retrieved (specimen C). Non-bleeding internal hemorrhoids were noted on retroflexion in the rectum, without stigmata of recent bleeding and insufficient to explain the presentation. No active arterial bleeding; contact bleeding from the sigmoid mass biopsy sites was self-limited. IMPRESSION: Ulcerated, partially obstructing sigmoid mass — suspicious for malignancy, biopsied and tattooed, pathology pending. Two colonic polyps removed. Incidental non-bleeding internal hemorrhoids. Findings likely account for the hematochezia; hemorrhoids are not the source. Awaiting histology.

findings.finding: Partially circumferential ulcerated friable mass, ~3.5 cm, mildly narrowing lumen, suspicious for malignancy
findings.action_taken: cold-forceps biopsies obtained; distal submucosal India ink tattoo placed for localization; not resected

4 mm sessile polyp

Patient counseled that a sigmoid lesion suspicious for cancer was found and biopsied, that results are pending, and that a staging and treatment plan will follow once pathology returns. Return precautions given for heavy bleeding, severe abdominal pain, fever, or obstructive symptoms.

followup.recommendation: Expedite and personally review sigmoid mass biopsy pathology (specimen A); contact patient with result and disclose the suspected diagnosis

Reconcile medication list: keep OTC ibuprofen held given GI bleeding and pending diagnosis; document fish oil as a supplement (not an anticoagulant). No prescription anticoagulant to resume
Document confirmed first-degree family history of colorectal cancer; will drive surveillance interval independent of index findings

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: first-time average-risk colorectal cancer screening in a 76-year-old, asymptomatic, no prior screening. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg), well tolerated; hemodynamically stable throughout with BP well controlled. PREP: adequate, BBPS 8/9. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. A single 6 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy; the specimen was retrieved intact and sent to pathology (specimen A). A 4 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy and retrieved (specimen B). Scattered small-mouthed diverticula were noted in the sigmoid colon without stigmata of recent bleeding. There was no mass, no ulceration, no luminal narrowing, and no active bleeding. Internal hemorrhoids noted on retroflexion, non-bleeding. IMPRESSION: 6 mm ascending colon polyp and 4 mm sigmoid polyp removed by cold snare; sigmoid diverticulosis; internal hemorrhoids. No malignancy identified. Pathology pending.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: surveillance for prior tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia (2021), interval due 2026. PERI-PROCEDURE ANTICOAGULATION: Patient on warfarin 5 mg daily for atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc 4). Warfarin held 5 days pre-procedure per anticoagulation clinic plan; INR checked morning of procedure = 1.2 (acceptable for polypectomy). Bridging with LMWH deferred given adequate INR normalization and endoscopic bleeding risk; resumption plan documented below. SEDATION: moderate. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. A 7 mm sessile polyp was identified in the sigmoid colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved (specimen A). A 4 mm sessile polyp in the transverse colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved (specimen B). Scattered sigmoid diverticula were noted without stigmata of bleeding. No active bleeding at polypectomy sites; hemostasis was confirmed. The remainder of the examined colon was normal. IMPRESSION: 7 mm sigmoid polyp and 4 mm transverse polyp removed by cold snare; sigmoid diverticulosis. Pathology pending. Peri-procedure warfarin held with therapeutic reversal by INR; resumption instructions given.

procedure.name: Colonoscopy
procedure.indication: Positive screening FIT (2026-06-15); asymptomatic — diagnostic colonoscopy on the FIT-positive pathway
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 100 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8/9 — adequate (right 3, transverse 2, left 3)
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with polypectomy. INDICATION: positive screening FIT (2026-06-15) in an asymptomatic 63-year-old man; FIT-positive diagnostic pathway. PERIPROCEDURAL: OTC aspirin 81 mg daily continued through the procedure (low-dose ASA does not require interruption for colonoscopy/polypectomy). SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl), tolerated well. PREP: adequate, BBPS 8/9. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with confirmation of the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. A 9 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by hot snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved (Specimen A). A 5 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved (Specimen B). A 3 mm rectal polyp was removed by cold forceps (Specimen C). Scattered non-complicated diverticula were noted in the sigmoid colon without stigmata of bleeding. There was no mass, no ulceration, and no source of active bleeding to explain the positive FIT; the polyps are the presumptive source. Hemostasis adequate at all polypectomy sites; a single prophylactic hemoclip was placed at the ascending colon site given continued aspirin. IMPRESSION: Three polyps removed (9 mm ascending, 5 mm sigmoid, 3 mm rectal); sigmoid diverticulosis. No malignancy or bleeding source identified. Pathology pending.

findings.finding: 9 mm sessile polyp
findings.action_taken: hot snare polypectomy, retrieved; prophylactic hemoclip placed

findings.finding: 5 mm sessile polyp
3 mm polyp
Diverticulosis, no stigmata of bleeding

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)
procedure.indication: Persistent GERD labeled refractory to PPI; evaluate for erosive esophagitis and Barrett's esophagus. No alarm features (no dysphagia, weight loss, or GI bleeding).
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy); NPO >8 hours, adequate mucosal visualization
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum (D2)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy. INDICATION: persistent GERD, rule out erosive disease/Barrett's; ongoing regular NSAID (ibuprofen) use noted. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg). The endoscope was passed under direct visualization from the mouth to the second portion of the duodenum. ESOPHAGUS: Several longitudinal mucosal breaks were seen in the distal esophagus, non-confluent, involving more than one mucosal fold — consistent with LA Grade B erosive esophagitis. The squamocolumnar junction was at 39 cm and the diaphragmatic hiatus at 42 cm, consistent with a small (approximately 3 cm) sliding hiatal hernia. No definite salmon-colored columnar mucosa suggestive of Barrett's above the gastroesophageal junction; no mass, no stricture, and the scope traversed the GE junction without resistance. STOMACH: Mild antral erythema consistent with gastritis; no ulcer, no mass. Retroflexion normal. DUODENUM: Bulb and D2 normal; no ulcer or erosion. BIOPSIES: Distal esophageal biopsies obtained to evaluate the mucosal breaks and exclude intestinal metaplasia (specimen A); gastric antral biopsies obtained for histology/H. pylori (specimen B). No active bleeding. IMPRESSION: LA Grade B erosive esophagitis, small hiatal hernia, mild antral gastritis. Findings consistent with undertreated acid reflux compounded by NSAID use. Pathology pending.

findings.finding: Erosive esophagitis, LA Grade B (non-confluent mucosal breaks, distal esophagus)
Small sliding hiatal hernia (~3 cm)
No endoscopic evidence of Barrett's esophagus
Mild antral gastritis (erythema, no ulcer)

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Tolerated procedure well. Post-sedation and diet instructions reviewed.

followup.recommendation: Review distal esophageal biopsy pathology to confirm reflux esophagitis and exclude intestinal metaplasia (Barrett's); communicate result to patient
Review gastric antral biopsy pathology (histology/H. pylori); treat if positive
Optimize acid suppression: pantoprazole 40 mg BID taken 30-60 minutes before meals, reinforce adherence; discontinue/minimize ibuprofen and substitute acetaminophen
Repeat EGD in 8-12 weeks after optimized PPI therapy to confirm mucosal healing of LA Grade B esophagitis and to assess the GE junction for Barrett's on a healed mucosa

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with biopsy
procedure.indication: Progressive solid-food dysphagia with ~15 lb weight loss and tobacco/alcohol risk factors (alarm features); expedited EGD to evaluate for obstructing esophageal lesion
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (MAC) with propofol; anesthesia involved given heavier-than-charted alcohol use and airway/sedation risk
procedure.prep_quality: NPO adequate; not applicable (upper endoscopy)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy. INDICATION: progressive solid-food dysphagia with weight loss and smoking/alcohol history (alarm features). SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care with propofol; tolerated well. PERIPROCEDURAL: OTC aspirin 81 mg was held for 5 days prior per plan given anticipated biopsies; fish oil (patient-described 'blood thinner') correctly identified as a supplement and did not require hold or delay. FINDINGS: The scope was passed under direct vision. The proximal and mid esophagus were normal. In the distal esophagus, beginning approximately 32 cm from the incisors and extending to the gastroesophageal junction at ~38 cm, there was a partially circumferential, friable, ulcerated mass causing luminal narrowing and mucosal irregularity. The adult gastroscope traversed the narrowed segment with mild resistance. Cold-forceps biopsies (x8) were obtained from the mass and sent to pathology. No active bleeding after biopsy; the ulcerated surface oozed minimally and stopped spontaneously. The stomach was examined in forward and retroflexed views; no esophageal or gastric varices were seen. The duodenum to the second portion was normal. IMPRESSION: Obstructing, ulcerated distal esophageal / GE-junction mass, highly suspicious for malignancy. Biopsies obtained; pathology pending. No varices. RECOMMENDATIONS: expedite pathology; oncologic staging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and EUS) and multidisciplinary referral pending tissue diagnosis.

findings.finding: Partially circumferential, friable, ulcerated obstructing mass, suspicious for malignancy
findings.location: distal esophagus / gastroesophageal junction (32-38 cm from incisors)

Address nutrition given dysphagia and ongoing weight loss (dietitian; consider enteral access if intake inadequate)

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with esophageal dilation
procedure.indication: Recurrent solid-food dysphagia from known distal peptic esophageal stricture (last dilated to 15 mm May 2025)
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesia-administered
procedure.prep_quality: NPO since midnight — adequate for upper endoscopy
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: EGD with esophageal dilation. INDICATION: recurrent solid-food dysphagia, known distal peptic esophageal stricture. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. PERIPROCEDURAL ANTICOAGULATION: apixaban 2.5 mg BID (corrected dose) held x 3 days pre-procedure per current CrCl obtained before scheduling; daily aspirin 81 mg held x 5 days in coordination with cardiology. Last apixaban dose documented prior to hold. FINDINGS: The esophagus demonstrated a smooth, tapered narrowing in the distal esophagus consistent with recurrent peptic stricture, luminal diameter approximately 10-11 mm, not comfortably traversable with the standard adult gastroscope on first pass. Mild distal esophagitis, LA grade A. Savary-Gilliard bougie dilation was performed in graded fashion to 15 mm with good effect and a positive dilation sign; the scope was subsequently passed easily. No mucosal tear beyond expected dilation effect, minimal self-limited oozing, no active bleeding at completion. The stomach and examined duodenum to the second portion were normal. Mucosal biopsies were obtained from the distal esophagus/stricture (specimen A) to exclude eosinophilic esophagitis and malignancy given recurrence, and to sample the LA grade A esophagitis. IMPRESSION: Recurrent distal peptic esophageal stricture, dilated to 15 mm with good result; LA grade A esophagitis. No malignancy grossly. Biopsies pending. No immediate complications.

findings.action_taken: Savary-Gilliard bougie dilation to 15 mm, good effect

findings.finding: Reflux esophagitis, LA grade A
Normal stomach and duodenum to D2

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with duodenal biopsy
procedure.indication: Serology-suspected celiac disease (tTG-IgA >10x ULN, IgA-sufficient) with iron deficiency; confirmatory duodenal biopsy on a gluten-containing diet
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg); topical pharyngeal anesthesia
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy); patient NPO x 8 hours, adequate fasting
procedure.extent_reached: Second/third portion of duodenum (post-bulbar); esophagus, stomach, and duodenum fully examined

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy. INDICATION: markedly elevated tTG-IgA (>10x ULN, IgA-sufficient) with iron deficiency; confirmatory duodenal biopsy. Patient confirmed to have continued a gluten-containing diet up to the procedure. SEDATION: moderate. FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed under direct vision. ESOPHAGUS: normal mucosa, no esophagitis, normal Z-line, no varices. STOMACH: normal gastric mucosa on forward and retroflexed views; no ulcer or mass; antrum normal. DUODENUM: the duodenal bulb and second/third portions were examined. Mucosa showed scalloping of the folds, mosaic/fissured pattern, and reduced fold height in the distal duodenum, findings suggestive of villous atrophy consistent with celiac disease. No ulcer, mass, or active bleeding. Biopsies obtained: four specimens from the second/third portion of the duodenum (distal) and two from the duodenal bulb, submitted separately per Marsh-protocol sampling. IMPRESSION: Endoscopic findings suggestive of villous atrophy (scalloping, mosaic pattern) consistent with celiac disease; duodenal biopsies obtained for confirmation. No bleeding source or mass to explain iron deficiency identified on upper exam. Pathology pending.

findings.finding: Duodenal mucosal scalloping, mosaic/fissured pattern, reduced fold height suggestive of villous atrophy
Duodenal bulb mucosa mildly abnormal
Normal esophageal mucosa, no esophagitis or varices
Normal gastric mucosa, no ulcer or mass on forward and retroflexed views

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Tolerated procedure well, no immediate complications. Post-endoscopy and sedation precautions reviewed.

followup.recommendation: Review duodenal biopsy pathology (Marsh grade); call patient with result to confirm or exclude celiac disease and update problem list from suspected to confirmed/excluded

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with variceal band ligation
procedure.indication: Variceal screening in NASH cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B); single episode of melena ~2 weeks prior raising concern for possible prior variceal bleed
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (anesthesiology-directed propofol) given Child-Pugh B cirrhosis, thrombocytopenia, possible hepatic encephalopathy, and documented history of severe PONV / delayed emergence with prior anesthesia
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy) — NPO status adequate; clear oropharynx
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with esophageal variceal band ligation. INDICATION: variceal screening in NASH cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B); reported single melenic stool ~2 weeks prior. PRE-PROCEDURE: Platelets 88k and INR 1.4 reviewed and attributed to cirrhotic synthetic dysfunction/hypersplenism, not anticoagulation; patient on no prescription antiplatelet/anticoagulant (OTC fish oil only, not held). Anesthesia-directed MAC used given comorbidity and prior adverse anesthesia history. SEDATION: MAC (propofol), well tolerated. FINDINGS: The esophagus demonstrated three columns of medium-to-large (grade 2-3) esophageal varices extending from the mid-esophagus to the gastroesophageal junction, with red wale marks on two columns indicating high risk of bleeding. No active bleeding; a small pigmented (flat, healed) area suggestive of a prior bleeding stigma was noted near the GE junction without active oozing. Four endoscopic band ligations were applied to the larger, high-risk columns with good deployment. The stomach showed a mosaic mucosal pattern consistent with mild-to-moderate portal hypertensive gastropathy; no gastric varices, no ulcer, no active bleeding. The examined duodenum to the second portion was normal. IMPRESSION: Medium-to-large esophageal varices with high-risk stigmata (red wale marks) and a healed bleeding stigma, consistent with the reported prior melena; four bands placed. Portal hypertensive gastropathy. Plan for repeat EGD banding sessions until variceal eradication and initiation of non-selective beta-blocker.

findings.finding: Grade 2-3 esophageal varices (3 columns) with red wale marks (high-risk stigmata)
Flat pigmented spot suggestive of healed/prior bleeding stigma, no active oozing
Portal hypertensive gastropathy (mild-to-moderate, mosaic pattern)
Normal duodenum to second portion

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with esophageal biopsies
procedure.indication: Recurrent esophageal food (bolus) impaction and lifelong solid-food dysphagia in a highly atopic young man; evaluate for eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE protocol biopsies)
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesia-assisted
procedure.prep_quality: NPO >8 hours; no retained food or secretions
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with esophageal biopsies (EoE protocol). INDICATION: recurrent esophageal food impaction and long-standing solid-food dysphagia; evaluate for eosinophilic esophagitis. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed under direct vision into the esophagus. The esophageal mucosa demonstrated classic features of eosinophilic esophagitis: multiple fixed concentric rings (trachealization) throughout the mid and distal esophagus, diffuse longitudinal linear furrows, scattered white exudates/plaques, and decreased vascular pattern consistent with edema (EREFS: Edema 1, Rings 2, Exudates 1, Furrows 2, Stricture 1). A focal area of narrowing was noted in the mid esophagus; the standard adult gastroscope traversed it with mild resistance and no significant mucosal tear. No retained food bolus. Given a stable, non-flush-limiting caliber and plan to confirm histology and initiate anti-inflammatory therapy first, no dilation was performed this session. EoE protocol biopsies were obtained: >=6 fragments from the proximal/mid esophagus and distal esophagus in separate jars, plus gastric and duodenal biopsies to exclude other causes of eosinophilia. The stomach and examined duodenum were normal with no evidence of eosinophilic gastroenteritis on gross inspection. No active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Endoscopic findings highly consistent with eosinophilic esophagitis (rings, furrows, exudates) with a mild non-obstructing mid-esophageal stricture/narrow-caliber esophagus. Histology pending. NOTE FOR PATHOLOGY: patient self-started OTC omeprazole 20 mg daily ~3 weeks before the procedure and did NOT hold it pre-procedure — active PPI exposure may attenuate the tissue eosinophil count and must be considered in interpretation.

Diffuse linear furrows and white exudates/plaques
Mild non-obstructing mid-esophageal stricture / narrow-caliber esophagus

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy. INDICATION: suspected UGIB (single coffee-ground emesis, resolved) in an 84-year-old on rivaroxaban with recent daily OTC ibuprofen; interval Hgb decrease. ANTICOAGULATION: rivaroxaban held for 4 days pre-procedure after last dose 2026-07-18 (renal dosing 15 mg daily per cardiology, CKD stage 3); no bridging given afib with acceptable interim risk. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. FINDINGS: The esophagus was normal with a normal Z-line, no esophagitis or varices. In the gastric antrum along the greater curvature there was a single 12 mm ulcer with a discrete adherent pigmented spot at one margin (Forrest IIc); no active bleeding, no visible vessel, and no adherent clot. Surrounding mucosa showed erythematous, erosive gastropathy consistent with NSAID injury. Cold-forceps biopsies were taken from the ulcer margin (malignancy exclusion) and separately from the gastric antrum/body for H. pylori. The duodenal bulb and second portion were normal. No stigmata of recent bleeding beyond the antral ulcer. IMPRESSION: 12 mm benign-appearing antral ulcer, Forrest IIc (adherent pigmented spot, low rebleeding risk), with diffuse NSAID-associated erosive gastropathy — likely culprit for the coffee-ground emesis. No high-risk stigmata requiring endoscopic hemostasis. Ulcer-edge and H. pylori biopsies pending.

findings.finding: 12 mm gastric ulcer with a marginal adherent pigmented spot (Forrest IIc), no active bleeding and no visible vessel
findings.action_taken: ulcer-margin biopsies for malignancy exclusion; no endoscopic hemostasis required

findings.finding: Erosive, erythematous gastropathy consistent with NSAID injury
Normal esophagus, no varices or esophagitis
Normal duodenal bulb and second portion

Return precautions (hematemesis, melena, lightheadedness) reviewed with daughter.

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with gastric biopsy and H. pylori culture/susceptibility
procedure.indication: Treatment-refractory Helicobacter pylori infection with persistent dyspepsia; gastric biopsy for culture/susceptibility to guide next-line eradication
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg) with topical pharyngeal anesthesia
procedure.prep_quality: NPO >8 hours; adequate mucosal visualization. Pantoprazole held 2 weeks pre-procedure per plan.
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum (D2)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with gastric biopsies. INDICATION: refractory H. pylori infection, biopsy for culture and susceptibility. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl). The endoscope was passed under direct vision to the second portion of the duodenum. ESOPHAGUS: normal mucosa; Z-line at 40 cm, no esophagitis, no Barrett's, no stricture or mass. STOMACH: mild-to-moderate erythematous, non-erosive antral gastritis with a nodular appearance of the antral mucosa consistent with H. pylori-associated changes. No gastric ulcer, no mass, no bleeding, no stigmata of recent hemorrhage. Rugal folds normal. Retroflexion of the cardia and fundus unremarkable. DUODENUM: normal bulb and D2, no ulceration. Targeted biopsies obtained per Sydney protocol (antrum x2, incisura x1, body x2) — a subset submitted fresh for H. pylori culture and antimicrobial susceptibility, the remainder in formalin for histology. Rapid urease test (CLO) performed intra-procedurally. IMPRESSION: Nodular non-erosive antral gastritis consistent with persistent H. pylori infection; no ulcer, mass, or bleeding source. Gastric biopsies sent for histology and culture/susceptibility.

findings.finding: Nodular non-erosive antral gastritis with mucosal erythema, consistent with H. pylori
findings.action_taken: biopsies obtained (Sydney protocol); subset for culture/susceptibility

pathology.report: Rapid urease (CLO) test POSITIVE intra-procedurally. Histology and H. pylori culture with antimicrobial susceptibility pending (culture typically 10-14 days).

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Tolerated procedure well. Instructed to remain off antibiotics until susceptibility-guided regimen is selected; may resume symptom control per clinic instructions.

procedure.name: Colonoscopy with polypectomy
procedure.indication: Surveillance colonoscopy (2023 ascending-colon 15 mm tubulovillous adenoma with low-grade dysplasia, piecemeal EMR with clip; 3-year interval due 2026). High-complexity peri-procedural anticoagulation — mechanical mitral valve.
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesia-assisted given cardiac comorbidity
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8/9 — adequate (right 3, transverse 3, left 2)
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated
procedure.anticoagulation_management: Warfarin held 5 days pre-procedure with therapeutic enoxaparin bridging per cardiology/anticoagulation clinic (mechanical MITRAL valve — no standard hold). Last LMWH dose >24 h pre-procedure; INR confirmed 1.3 on day of procedure. OTC aspirin 81 mg held 5 days pre-procedure per cardiology. Post-polypectomy resumption plan documented (see follow-up).

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with polypectomy. INDICATION: surveillance (prior 15 mm ascending-colon tubulovillous adenoma with low-grade dysplasia, piecemeal EMR 2023) in a patient with a mechanical mitral valve on warfarin. ANTICOAGULATION: warfarin held with therapeutic enoxaparin bridging per cardiology; INR 1.3 day-of; OTC aspirin held. SEDATION: MAC/propofol, anesthesia-assisted. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. In the ascending colon, the prior EMR resection site was identified by a residual clip and mucosal scar; the scar was carefully inspected under high-definition white light and narrow-band imaging, biopsied, and tattooed distally for future localization — no visible residual or recurrent adenomatous tissue. A single 6 mm sessile polyp was found in the transverse colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy, retrieved. A 4 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy, retrieved. Scattered sigmoid diverticula without stigmata of bleeding. No mass, no active bleeding. Resection sites inspected with no immediate bleeding; given anticoagulation status, prophylactic hemostatic clips were placed at the transverse resection base. IMPRESSION: (1) Prior ascending-colon EMR scar — no visible residual, biopsied and tattooed; (2) 6 mm transverse polyp and 4 mm sigmoid polyp removed by cold snare; (3) diverticulosis. Pathology pending.

findings.action_taken: high-definition/NBI inspection, biopsy of scar, tattoo placed distally

Must be cardiology/anticoag-clinic co-managed, not a standard protocol.

procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated
procedure.periprocedural_antithrombotic: Coordinated with cardiology prior to procedure given drug-eluting stents placed Dec 2025 (<12 months). Aspirin 81 mg CONTINUED throughout. Ticagrelor 90 mg BID held for 3 days pre-procedure per cardiology instruction; cardiology approved short hold with plan to resume promptly. No bridging. INR 1.0; not on systemic anticoagulant.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with polypectomy, endoscopic mucosal resection, and prophylactic clip placement. INDICATION: Positive FIT plus new intermittent hematochezia and mild anemia in a 62-year-old man; high pretest concern for a colonic source. ANTITHROMBOTIC: High-risk cardiac patient with two drug-eluting stents (Dec 2025) on dual antiplatelet therapy. Periprocedural plan coordinated with cardiology: aspirin continued; ticagrelor held 3 days pre-procedure with cardiology approval and a plan for prompt resumption. SEDATION: MAC/propofol, anesthesia-directed. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. In the sigmoid colon a 22 mm laterally spreading, granular sessile lesion was identified; it was lifted with submucosal injection and removed piecemeal by endoscopic mucosal resection. The resection base appeared complete; two prophylactic hemostatic clips were placed given continued aspirin and pending ticagrelor resumption. A separate 6 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon was removed by cold snare. Moderate sigmoid diverticulosis was present without active bleeding or stigmata. Small non-bleeding internal hemorrhoids were noted on retroflexion in the rectum. No active bleeding at completion; adequate hemostasis confirmed. IMPRESSION: (1) 22 mm sigmoid laterally spreading lesion removed by piecemeal EMR, clips placed — pathology pending, concerning for advanced adenoma; (2) 6 mm descending colon polyp removed; (3) diverticulosis; (4) small internal hemorrhoids (likely source of bright red blood on tissue). The sigmoid lesion is the probable source of the positive FIT and anemia. Pathology pending. Delayed post-polypectomy bleeding risk elevated on continued aspirin and resumed antiplatelet therapy.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: surveillance for prior tubular adenoma with low-grade dysplasia (ascending colon, 2023). PATIENT: 69-year-old woman, asymptomatic. ANTICOAGULATION: patient not on anticoagulation — completed provoked-DVT apixaban course and discontinued ~January 2026 (reconciled at pre-procedure consult); no periprocedural hold or bridging required. SEDATION: moderate. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum; the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve were identified and the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. A single 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy; the specimen was retrieved and sent to pathology (specimen A). Mild diverticulosis was noted in the sigmoid colon without stigmata of bleeding. No masses, no strictures, no ulceration. The mucosa of the remainder of the examined colon was normal. No active bleeding at the polypectomy site after removal. IMPRESSION: 5 mm ascending colon polyp removed by cold snare; mild sigmoid diverticulosis; otherwise normal surveillance colonoscopy to the cecum with terminal ileal intubation. Pathology pending.

procedure.indication: Anemia with new melena / possible GI blood loss in an anticoagulated CKD4 patient (iron status unresolved: iron deficiency vs. anemia of CKD) — evaluation for GI bleeding source
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol, anesthesia-directed) given CKD4 and periprocedural bleeding risk
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated
procedure.anticoagulation_note: Dabigatran (75 mg BID, renally cleared, CKD4/eGFR 22) held on an extended schedule coordinated with nephrology and anesthesia per pre-procedure plan; over-the-counter fish oil also held. No anticoagulant taken periprocedurally. Resumption deliberately DEFERRED pending confirmation of hemostasis and pathology given active occult bleeding source — see follow-up.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with cold-forceps biopsy. INDICATION: anemia with new melena / possible occult GI blood loss (iron status unresolved) in a CKD4 patient anticoagulated for atrial fibrillation. SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care. PREP: adequate (BBPS 7). ANTICOAGULATION: dabigatran and fish oil held on an extended, nephrology/anesthesia-coordinated schedule; none taken periprocedurally. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. In the ascending colon a 3.5 cm partially obstructing, friable, ulcerated mass was identified with contact bleeding and adherent clot; no active spurting. Multiple cold-forceps biopsies were obtained (specimen A) and the site was tattooed with sterile carbon marking distal to the lesion for future localization. Scattered non-bleeding angioectasias were noted in the right colon; the most prominent was treated with limited argon plasma coagulation (specimen not taken). A few sigmoid diverticula were seen without stigmata of bleeding. No other mucosal lesions. IMPRESSION: 1) Ascending colon mass, ulcerated and friable — suspicious for malignancy; biopsied and tattooed, pathology pending. 2) Right-colon angioectasias, one treated with APC. 3) Sigmoid diverticulosis. This lesion is a plausible source of occult/overt GI blood loss and the anemia. Pathology pending; oncology/surgical evaluation to follow biopsy results.

Return precautions for large-volume bleeding, dizziness, or worsening fatigue reviewed.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: average-risk colorectal cancer screening in a 57-year-old asymptomatic woman, first-time screening. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg), well tolerated. PREP: adequate, BBPS 8/9. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. A single 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed in its entirety by cold snare polypectomy; the specimen was retrieved and sent to pathology (Specimen A). A 3 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon was removed by cold biopsy forceps and retrieved (Specimen B). Scattered non-bleeding diverticula were noted in the sigmoid colon. The remainder of the examined colon and the rectum (including retroflexion) were normal. No masses, no ulceration, no active bleeding, no luminal narrowing. IMPRESSION: Two small colon polyps removed (5 mm ascending, 3 mm descending); sigmoid diverticulosis. Pathology pending. Overall low-risk screening examination.

procedure.name: Bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy)
procedure.indication: Iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 8.4), guaiac-positive stool, ~2 weeks of melena — evaluation of GI blood loss, upper source suspected
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesiologist-directed given age 81, HFpEF, CKD3a; opioid-sparing (documented codeine intolerance)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 — adequate (right 2, transverse 3, left 2)
procedure.extent_reached: EGD to second/third portion of duodenum; colonoscopy to cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve intubation)

procedure_report:
PROCEDURE: Bidirectional endoscopy — esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) followed by colonoscopy, same session. INDICATION: symptomatic iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 8.4 g/dL), guaiac-positive stool, and ~2 weeks of melena; suspected upper GI blood loss. ANTICOAGULATION: patient on edoxaban (dose corrected to 30 mg daily); last dose held per periprocedural plan coordinated with cardiology; procedure performed on held DOAC. SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care (propofol). 

EGD FINDINGS: The esophagus was normal with a normal Z-line; no varices. The stomach was examined in forward and retroflexed views; no fundic or antral mass. In the duodenal bulb there was a single 12 mm ulcer with a non-bleeding visible vessel (Forrest IIa) and adherent clot. The clot was removed by targeted irrigation; combination endoscopic hemostasis was performed — dilute epinephrine injection (1:10,000) at the ulcer base followed by placement of two through-the-scope hemostatic clips to the visible vessel. Hemostasis was achieved with no active bleeding at completion. Antral and body biopsies were obtained to assess for H. pylori (rapid urease test placed; histology sent). The ulcer edge was not biopsied at this setting given recent hemostasis; interval healing EGD planned. 

COLONOSCOPY FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum. Moderate sigmoid diverticulosis without stigmata of recent bleeding. A single 6 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy and retrieved (specimen B). Terminal ileum intubated and normal. No colonic mass, no active bleeding source identified. 

IMPRESSION: Bleeding duodenal (bulbar) ulcer, Forrest IIa — successful endoscopic hemostasis (epinephrine + 2 clips); most likely source of melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Gastric biopsies for H. pylori (pending). 6 mm ascending colon polyp removed. Sigmoid diverticulosis. Start high-dose PPI.

findings.finding: Duodenal bulb ulcer, 12 mm, non-bleeding visible vessel with adherent clot (Forrest IIa)
findings.action_taken: clot removed; dilute epinephrine injection + 2 hemostatic clips; hemostasis achieved

disposition: Admitted for overnight observation given high-risk ulcer (Forrest IIa), symptomatic anemia, and DOAC. IV pantoprazole (80 mg bolus then infusion), NPO then advanced. Type and screen; transfusion threshold Hgb <7 or if symptomatic (1 unit PRBC given periprocedurally for Hgb 7.6 with dyspnea). Hemodynamically stable at end of procedure.

Continue IV iron repletion and monitor CBC; recheck hemoglobin

procedure.name: Colonoscopy (surveillance) — DEFERRED, not performed
procedure.indication: Surveillance colonoscopy — history of two tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia (2021); interval due 2026. Asymptomatic; no red-flag symptoms.
procedure.sedation: Not administered — procedure deferred.
procedure.prep_quality: N/A — procedure not performed.
procedure.extent_reached: N/A — procedure not performed.
procedure.antithrombotic_management: Patient is ~2 months post drug-eluting stent to the mid RCA (2026-05-14) on dual antiplatelet therapy (ticagrelor 90 mg BID + aspirin 81 mg daily). Per interview she self-discontinued the aspirin approximately 2 weeks ago (believed she did not need 'two blood thinners'); cardiology was NOT informed. This is an unsanctioned interruption of a DAPT component early after DES — stent-thrombosis risk. Ticagrelor continues. No cardiology clearance for an elective scope/polypectomy has been obtained; cardiology consult is mandatory before any endoscopy or polypectomy.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE DEFERRED — NOT PERFORMED. This was a pre-procedure evaluation for an elective surveillance colonoscopy (prior tubular adenomas, 2021). On interview the patient is entirely asymptomatic (no hematochezia, melena, pain, or change in bowel habits), so this is routine surveillance rather than an acute evaluation. The referral had been flagged urgent; reclassified to elective. The dominant issue is cardiac: NSTEMI on 2026-05-14 treated with a drug-eluting stent to the mid RCA (~2 months ago) with the patient on dual antiplatelet therapy. Performing an elective colonoscopy with polypectomy within the first months post-DES on DAPT carries high stent-thrombosis and bleeding risk. In addition, the patient reports self-discontinuing her aspirin ~2 weeks ago without informing cardiology, an unsanctioned interruption of a DAPT component. DECISION: defer the elective colonoscopy. Recommend rescheduling to approximately month 12 post-stent (~mid-2027), contingent on mandatory cardiology consultation to define acceptable timing and peri-procedural antiplatelet management. No scope was introduced; no sedation given; no polyps removed. Cardiology to be contacted today regarding the aspirin interruption and resumption plan. Codeine intolerance (nausea/vomiting) newly reported and to be documented.
disposition: Procedure deferred; patient discharged from clinic in stable condition. Counseled not to stop or restart antiplatelet agents on her own and to await cardiology guidance before resuming aspirin. Elective surveillance colonoscopy to be rescheduled after cardiology clearance (~1 year post-stent). Return precautions for chest pain/pressure or shortness of breath reviewed (contact cardiology or seek emergency care).

followup.recommendation: Obtain mandatory cardiology consultation before any elective endoscopy/polypectomy to define acceptable timing and peri-procedural antiplatelet management post-DES

URGENT: message cardiology today regarding patient's self-discontinuation of aspirin ~2 weeks ago (unsanctioned DAPT interruption ~2 months post-DES); obtain guidance on resuming aspirin before patient restarts on her own
Reschedule elective surveillance colonoscopy to approximately month 12 post-stent (~mid-2027) once cardiology clears timing
Document codeine intolerance (nausea/vomiting) in the chart; reinforce ticagrelor is an antiplatelet, not an anticoagulant/warfarin

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: positive screening FIT (2026-06-24) in a 65-year-old asymptomatic man; no anemia (Hgb 14.6). Low-dose aspirin continued periprocedurally per plan given remote bare-metal coronary stent (2014); no therapeutic anticoagulant on board. SEDATION: moderate. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. A 7 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved and sent to pathology. A 4 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved. Scattered non-bleeding diverticula were noted in the sigmoid colon. No mass, no ulceration, and no source of active bleeding was identified. Hemostasis was intact at both polypectomy sites; no bleeding despite continued aspirin. IMPRESSION: Two colon polyps removed (7 mm ascending, 4 mm descending); sigmoid diverticulosis. FIT positivity most likely attributable to the polyps. Pathology pending.

Return precautions given so a true bleed would be caught.

procedure.name: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
procedure.indication: Choledocholithiasis (7 mm distal CBD stone on MRCP) following resolved acute gallstone pancreatitis; duct clearance prior to interval cholecystectomy
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (MAC) with propofol, anesthesiologist-administered
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (biliary procedure)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum; major papilla identified and cannulated selectively into the common bile duct

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy, occlusion cholangiogram, and balloon stone extraction. INDICATION: choledocholithiasis with mild upstream dilation on MRCP; resolved gallstone pancreatitis; downtrending LFTs. PREMEDICATION: given documented prior pruritic reaction to IV iodinated contrast, patient received corticosteroid and antihistamine premedication per protocol and anesthesia was notified; low-osmolar contrast used. Aspirin 81 mg was continued per proceduralist (acceptable for sphincterotomy); OTC fish oil supplement had been held pre-procedure; no prescription anticoagulant on board. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. FINDINGS: The duodenoscope was advanced to the major papilla without difficulty. The major papilla appeared normal. The common bile duct was selectively cannulated with a sphincterotome and guidewire. Occlusion cholangiogram demonstrated a mildly dilated CBD (~9 mm) with a single small (~4-5 mm) filling defect in the distal duct and a small amount of sludge — smaller than the 7 mm stone reported on prior MRCP, consistent with interval partial passage. A standard biliary sphincterotomy was performed with good bile flow. The duct was swept with an extraction balloon on multiple passes, retrieving one small stone and biliary sludge. Repeat occlusion cholangiogram showed no residual filling defects and free flow of contrast into the duodenum. Given the recent pancreatitis and instrumentation, a prophylactic 5 Fr x 5 cm pancreatic duct stent was placed to reduce post-ERCP pancreatitis risk, with good drainage. No immediate bleeding at the sphincterotomy site. IMPRESSION: Choledocholithiasis with successful duct clearance after sphincterotomy and balloon extraction; residual stone burden smaller than on prior imaging (interval partial passage). Prophylactic pancreatic duct stent placed. Technical success. Plan for interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

procedure.name: ERCP with sphincterotomy, stone extraction, and biliary stent placement
procedure.indication: Acute ascending cholangitis with suspected choledocholithiasis (CBD 12 mm, total bilirubin 6.2 mg/dL) — urgent/emergent biliary decompression
procedure.sedation: General endotracheal anesthesia given emergent status, sepsis, and aspiration risk
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopic procedure)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; major papilla identified and cannulated; biliary tree opacified to the intrahepatic ducts

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy, balloon stone extraction, and plastic biliary stent placement. INDICATION: acute cholangitis secondary to choledocholithiasis, emergent decompression. ANESTHESIA: general endotracheal (emergent, septic patient). PERIPROCEDURAL: Patient on apixaban 5 mg BID for atrial fibrillation with last dose today ~07:00 (near-peak effect). Per multidisciplinary discussion with anesthesia and pharmacy, given life-threatening cholangitis requiring emergent decompression, procedure proceeded without delay; apixaban held and 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) administered pre-procedure for reversal prior to sphincterotomy. Documented iodinated contrast reaction (hives) — premedicated with corticosteroids and antihistamine; low-osmolar contrast used with minimized volume. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and fluids continued. FINDINGS: The duodenoscope was advanced to the major papilla without difficulty. The papilla appeared bulging/edematous consistent with an impacted stone. The bile duct was deeply cannulated. Cholangiogram demonstrated a dilated common bile duct to 12 mm with a single obstructing 9 mm filling defect in the distal CBD and intrahepatic biliary dilation. A biliary sphincterotomy was performed with controlled cut; hemostasis was intact with no post-sphincterotomy bleeding. Balloon sweep of the duct extracted one pigmented stone with prompt drainage of purulent bile (frank pus), confirming cholangitis. Occlusion cholangiogram showed no residual filling defects. A 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic biliary stent was placed across the sphincterotomy to ensure ongoing drainage. IMPRESSION: Successful emergent biliary decompression — sphincterotomy, extraction of one CBD stone, drainage of purulent bile, and biliary stent placement. Technically successful. No immediate complication.

Purulent bile (frank pus) on duct drainage — confirms suppurative cholangitis
Occlusion cholangiogram with no residual filling defect

procedure.name: EUS-FNA of pancreatic head mass with same-session ERCP and biliary stent placement

code.display: Endoscopic ultrasonography with fine needle aspiration (procedure)

procedure.indication: 3.2 cm pancreatic head mass with painless obstructive jaundice (total bilirubin 4.1) and CA 19-9 890; tissue diagnosis and biliary decompression
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (MAC) with propofol, anesthesiology-administered; prone/semi-prone positioning for ERCP
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopic procedure)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; pancreatic head fully interrogated by linear echoendoscope; major papilla cannulated at ERCP
procedure.periprocedural_notes: Metformin 500 mg BID held morning of procedure per NPO plan. Documented prior rash to iodinated IV contrast — premedicated with methylprednisolone and diphenhydramine per protocol; low-osmolar contrast used sparingly under fluoroscopy with no reaction. OTC fish oil (omega-3) continued; not a clinically significant anticoagulant and did not require holding. No therapeutic anticoagulant/antiplatelet on file.

procedure_report:
PROCEDURE: Linear EUS with fine needle aspiration of pancreatic head mass, followed by same-session ERCP with biliary stent placement. INDICATION: pancreatic head mass with obstructive jaundice; tissue diagnosis and decompression. SEDATION: MAC (propofol). CONTRAST ALLERGY: prior rash to iodinated contrast — premedicated (steroid + antihistamine); minimal low-osmolar contrast used without reaction.

EUS FINDINGS: A hypoechoic, ill-defined mass measuring approximately 3.3 x 3.0 cm was identified in the pancreatic head. The mass abutted the portal confluence with preserved fat plane; no definite vascular invasion of the SMA/SMV was seen on this exam. Upstream main pancreatic duct and common bile duct were dilated (double-duct sign). A rounded 9 mm peripancreatic lymph node was noted. No ascites. Liver segments surveyed showed no discrete lesion. FNA was performed with a 22-gauge needle under Doppler guidance, 4 passes, with rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) reporting atypical/malignant epithelial cells consistent with adenocarcinoma; additional passes collected for cell block and cytology.

ERCP FINDINGS: The major papilla was identified and cannulated selectively. Cholangiogram (limited contrast) demonstrated a tight ~2 cm distal common bile duct stricture at the level of the mass with marked upstream intra- and extrahepatic biliary dilation. A guidewire was advanced across the stricture. A 10 Fr x 9 cm plastic biliary stent was deployed across the stricture with brisk drainage of bile and contrast. Given the malignant appearance, metal stenting was deferred pending confirmed cytology and staging/resectability determination at tumor board. No sphincterotomy complication; prophylactic rectal indomethacin administered.

IMPRESSION: (1) Pancreatic head mass, ROSE positive for malignant cells — cytology/cell block pending. (2) Malignant-appearing distal CBD stricture, decompressed with plastic biliary stent — technically successful. (3) Suspicious 9 mm peripancreatic lymph node. Await final cytology and staging for MDT review.

procedure.name: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) of the pancreas, cyst protocol, with EUS-guided fine-needle aspiration
procedure.indication: Characterization of incidental 14 mm cystic lesion in the pancreatic body (found on trauma CT 2026-06-21); rule out mucinous/malignant features. Family history of pancreatic cancer (father).
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesiologist-directed
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper EUS; NPO x8h confirmed)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; complete survey of pancreatic head, body, and tail via transgastric and transduodenal windows

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Linear echoendoscope EUS of the pancreas with EUS-FNA. INDICATION: incidental 14 mm pancreatic body cystic lesion for characterization. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. FINDINGS: The echoendoscope was advanced to the second portion of the duodenum without difficulty. Systematic survey of the entire pancreas was performed. A single 13 x 12 mm anechoic, unilocular cystic lesion was identified in the pancreatic body with thin, smooth walls and no enhancing mural nodule, no internal septations, and no solid component. The main pancreatic duct measured 2 mm and was normal in caliber with no communication clearly demonstrated with the cyst. No upstream ductal dilation. No associated parenchymal mass. No peripancreatic lymphadenopathy. Remainder of the pancreatic parenchyma was normal in echotexture. Common bile duct normal caliber; no biliary dilation. Under real-time EUS guidance, a single-pass 22-gauge FNA of the cyst was performed transgastrically with near-complete aspiration of ~1.5 mL of clear, non-viscous fluid. Fluid was sent for CEA, amylase, cytology, and molecular (KRAS/GNAS) analysis. Prophylactic antibiotics administered per cyst-aspiration protocol. No immediate complication; no bleeding at the puncture site on Doppler. IMPRESSION: Solitary 13 mm unilocular pancreatic body cyst without high-risk stigmata (no mural nodule, no main-duct involvement, no solid mass). Benign-appearing morphology; FNA fluid analysis and cytology pending to differentiate serous vs. mucinous etiology.

findings.action_taken: EUS-FNA, 22G single pass, ~1.5 mL clear fluid aspirated

Common bile duct normal caliber, no biliary dilation

procedure.name: ERCP with plastic biliary stent exchange
procedure.indication: Scheduled (elective) exchange of indwelling plastic biliary stent for benign distal common bile duct stricture; asymptomatic, normal LFTs
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol) with anesthesiology present
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopic/biliary procedure)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; major papilla identified and cannulated; deep biliary cannulation achieved

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: ERCP with plastic biliary stent exchange. INDICATION: routine scheduled exchange of a 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic biliary stent placed 2026-04-16 for a benign distal CBD stricture; patient asymptomatic with normal LFTs. ANTIPLATELET MANAGEMENT: clopidogrel (single-agent for secondary stroke prevention after 2024 TIA) was CONTINUED without interruption — a simple plastic stent exchange without sphincterotomy is a low-bleeding-risk procedure; given the patient's stroke risk on monotherapy, no hold was indicated and this was confirmed with the endoscopist prior to the case. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. FINDINGS: The duodenoscope was advanced to the major papilla without difficulty. The previously placed plastic biliary stent was seen protruding from the papilla, patent and in good position; it was grasped with a snare and removed intact. There was good flow of clear bile after removal. Deep biliary cannulation was achieved over a guidewire without sphincterotomy. Occlusion cholangiogram demonstrated a short, smooth, benign-appearing distal CBD stricture, stable to modestly improved compared with the April 2026 study, with mild residual upstream ductal dilation and no filling defects/stones. Repeat brush cytology of the stricture was obtained. A new 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic biliary stent was placed across the stricture over the guidewire with brisk drainage of bile and good positioning. No sphincterotomy was performed. No immediate bleeding. IMPRESSION: Successful uncomplicated plastic biliary stent exchange for a stable benign distal CBD stricture. New 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic stent in good position. Repeat brush cytology pending. Clopidogrel continued throughout. Plan for next scheduled exchange in ~3 months and ongoing surveillance of the benign-appearing stricture.

procedure.name: Laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
procedure.indication: Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction (9 mm distal CBD calculus, CBD dilated to 11 mm) in a patient with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; standard per-oral ERCP not feasible in altered anatomy
procedure.sedation: General anesthesia with endotracheal intubation (combined operative/endoscopic case)
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (biliary procedure)
procedure.extent_reached: Major papilla accessed via laparoscopic gastrostomy in the excluded (remnant) stomach; duodenoscope passed through transgastric port to the second portion of the duodenum; deep selective biliary cannulation achieved

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy, balloon sweep, and stone extraction. INDICATION: choledocholithiasis with obstruction in Roux-en-Y gastric bypass anatomy; per-oral ERCP not feasible. ANESTHESIA: general endotracheal. APPROACH: Given altered anatomy and unavailable operative limb-length documentation, a laparoscopic-assisted transgastric approach was selected jointly with general/bariatric surgery. Laparoscopy was performed; the excluded remnant stomach was identified and a gastrotomy created, through which a 15 mm port was placed. The duodenoscope was introduced through the transgastric port and advanced to the major papilla in the second portion of the duodenum. FINDINGS: The major papilla was identified with a normal-appearing orifice. Deep selective biliary cannulation was achieved with a sphincterotome and wire. Cholangiogram demonstrated a dilated common bile duct (~11 mm) with a single ~9 mm filling defect in the distal CBD, consistent with the MRCP finding; no intrahepatic filling defects; pancreatic duct not opacified. A biliary sphincterotomy was performed. The stone was extracted with an extraction balloon; repeat balloon sweeps cleared the duct with no residual filling defects. Good drainage of bile and contrast was observed. A biliary stent was not required given complete duct clearance. The duodenoscope was withdrawn, the gastrotomy was closed by the surgical team, and the transgastric port site was closed. IMPRESSION: Successful laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP with sphincterotomy and complete extraction of a distal CBD stone; duct cleared. Residual cholelithiasis noted on prior imaging — gallbladder in situ. Peri-procedural note: reported daily 'blood thinner' clarified pre-operatively as over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3); no therapeutic anticoagulant, no periprocedural hold required.

findings.finding: Dilated common bile duct (~11 mm) with single ~9 mm distal CBD calculus on cholangiogram
findings.action_taken: biliary sphincterotomy, balloon extraction, duct cleared

pathology.report: Extracted material consists of brown/black pigmented calculus fragments; no epithelial tissue submitted. No cytologic atypia in accompanying biliary aspirate.

procedure.name: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), upper GI, with FNA

code.display: Endoscopic ultrasonography of upper gastrointestinal tract (procedure)

procedure.indication: Characterization of an 18 mm subepithelial gastric lesion identified on outside EGD; asymptomatic
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesia-directed
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy) — NPO >8 hours, adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; radial and linear echoendoscope examination of stomach completed

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) of the upper GI tract with fine-needle aspiration/biopsy. INDICATION: 18 mm subepithelial gastric lesion found on outside EGD; asymptomatic. SEDATION: MAC (propofol). FINDINGS: The echoendoscope was advanced without difficulty. Endoscopically, a smooth subepithelial bulge with intact overlying mucosa was seen along the gastric body/fundus (greater curve), without ulceration. On radial and linear EUS, a well-circumscribed, hypoechoic, homogeneous lesion measuring 19 x 16 mm was identified arising from the fourth echolayer (muscularis propria). No internal cystic spaces, calcification, or exophytic irregular borders. No peri-gastric lymphadenopathy; no ascites. Doppler confirmed avascular needle path. EUS-guided fine-needle biopsy was performed with a 22-gauge core needle x3 passes under real-time guidance; adequate cellular cores obtained on-site macroscopic assessment. No immediate bleeding at the puncture site. The examined esophagus, remainder of stomach, and duodenum to the second portion were normal. IMPRESSION: 19 mm hypoechoic lesion arising from the muscularis propria, most consistent with a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST); differential includes leiomyoma or schwannoma. EUS-FNB performed; histopathology and immunohistochemistry pending.

findings.finding: Hypoechoic homogeneous subepithelial lesion arising from muscularis propria (4th layer), consistent with GIST vs leiomyoma/schwannoma
No peri-gastric lymphadenopathy or ascites
Normal esophagus, remaining stomach, and duodenum to D2

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Post-EUS/FNB precautions reviewed; advised to report abdominal pain, fever, hematemesis, or melena.

followup.recommendation: Review EUS-FNB histopathology and immunohistochemistry (CD117/DOG-1/S100/desmin); communicate result to patient. If GIST confirmed, obtain staging CT and refer to surgical oncology / MDT for resection decision based on size and mitotic index
If pathology non-diagnostic or benign (e.g., leiomyoma), establish EUS surveillance interval for the subepithelial lesion
Reconcile lesion size against outside EGD report once received (referral 18 mm vs EUS 19 mm vs patient-recalled ~2 cm)
Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily

procedure.name: ERCP with cholangiography, dominant stricture assessment, biliary brushing cytology, and biliary stent placement
procedure.indication: PSC with new dominant stricture of the common hepatic duct and rising cholestatic labs (ALP 320 -> 480, total bilirubin 1.9); MRCP cannot exclude superimposed cholangiocarcinoma. Tissue sampling (brushings +/- FISH) to establish or exclude malignancy.
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol) with anesthesia support
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (biliary procedure); NPO confirmed, OTC fish oil held 5 days pre-procedure per instructions
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; major papilla identified and cannulated; selective deep biliary cannulation achieved

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: ERCP with cholangiography, biliary brushing cytology, and biliary stent placement. INDICATION: PSC with new dominant common hepatic duct stricture, rising cholestatic labs, and MRCP unable to exclude cholangiocarcinoma. SEDATION: MAC/propofol with anesthesia. ANTICOAGULATION: no prescription anticoagulant/antiplatelet; patient's OTC fish oil held pre-procedure; no reversal or bridging required. FINDINGS: The duodenoscope was advanced without difficulty to the major papilla. Selective deep biliary cannulation was achieved with a sphincterotome and guidewire. Cholangiography demonstrated the known PSC changes with multifocal intra- and extrahepatic strictures and beading. A dominant stricture of the common hepatic duct measuring approximately 2 cm in length was confirmed, with upstream intrahepatic ductal dilatation, corresponding to the MRCP finding and increased from prior. A biliary sphincterotomy was performed. The dominant stricture was traversed with a guidewire. Brush cytology of the dominant stricture was obtained with multiple passes (specimen sent for cytology and FISH). The stricture was dilated with a balloon, and a 10 Fr x 9 cm plastic biliary stent was placed across the dominant stricture with good bile drainage observed. No active bleeding at the sphincterotomy site. IMPRESSION: Dominant common hepatic duct stricture in PSC, brushed for cytology/FISH; balloon dilation and plastic biliary stent placed with good drainage. Cannot confirm or exclude cholangiocarcinoma at this time — awaiting cytology/FISH. Cytology and FISH pending.

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Post-ERCP precautions reviewed including return for fever/chills, jaundice, or severe abdominal pain (post-ERCP pancreatitis/cholangitis). Resume ursodiol and mesalamine as before.

followup.recommendation: Review dominant stricture brush cytology + FISH; cancer cannot be excluded — communicate result to patient and hepatology and determine oncologic vs surveillance pathway
If cytology/FISH negative or indeterminate but clinical suspicion persists, consider repeat brushings or cholangioscopy with directed biopsy
Plastic biliary stent placed — schedule stent exchange/removal to avoid occlusion/cholangitis
Monitor for post-ERCP pancreatitis and cholangitis; return precautions given
Resume OTC fish oil per instructions; no prescription anticoagulant to resume
Confirm mesalamine 1.2 g daily and ursodiol 300 mg TID continued; ensure mesalamine reflected on active medication list

procedure.indication: Surveillance colonoscopy — prior tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia (2021), surveillance due 2026
procedure.sedation: Anesthesia-directed monitored anesthesia care (propofol) in setting equipped for airway rescue; endotracheal intubation not required. Severe OSA (AHI 42) + BMI 41 with unverified CPAP adherence — anesthesia pre-op review completed; CPAP brought day-of and applied in recovery. Latex-safe environment used per reported latex contact allergy.
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 — adequate (right 2, transverse 2, left 3)
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with polypectomy. INDICATION: surveillance for prior tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia (2021). ANESTHESIA: anesthesia-directed monitored anesthesia care (propofol) given severe OSA (AHI 42), morbid obesity (BMI 41), and unverified/poor CPAP adherence; airway managed without intubation; patient's own CPAP applied in PACU. Latex-safe room per patient-reported latex contact allergy. PREP: adequate (BBPS 7). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with confirmation of the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. A 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy, specimen retrieved (Specimen A). A 4 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy, specimen retrieved (Specimen B). Scattered sigmoid diverticulosis without stigmata of bleeding. Internal hemorrhoids, non-bleeding. No mass, no ulceration, no active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Two small polyps removed (ascending 5 mm, sigmoid 4 mm); mild sigmoid diverticulosis; internal hemorrhoids. Pathology pending. PERI-PROCEDURE: Hemodynamically stable throughout; SpO2 maintained on supplemental O2 with close airway monitoring; no desaturation events. Recovered on CPAP in PACU without complication.

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with biopsy
procedure.indication: Progressive solid-food dysphagia with unintentional ~12 lb weight loss (alarm features)
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (anesthesiologist-administered propofol) in hospital-based endoscopy suite due to severe COPD on home O2, cor pulmonale, and reported prior sedation intolerance; supplemental O2 titrated, continuous capnography

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsies. INDICATION: progressive solid-food dysphagia with unintentional weight loss (alarm features). ANESTHESIA: MAC with anesthesia team present given high respiratory/cardiac risk; apixaban held for 3 days pre-procedure per cardiology/anesthesia guidance. FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed under direct vision. In the distal esophagus, extending from approximately 34 to 39 cm from the incisors, there was a firm, partially circumferential, ulcerated and friable mass causing luminal narrowing; the endoscope traversed the narrowing with mild resistance. No active bleeding, though the lesion was friable to contact. The mass extended distally to involve the gastroesophageal junction at its distal margin (~39 cm). The stomach was examined in forward and retroflexed views without additional mass; mild antral gastritis noted. The duodenum to the second portion was normal. Targeted biopsies (x8) were obtained from the esophageal mass. No dilation performed given need for staging and tissue diagnosis first. IMPRESSION: Distal esophageal / GE-junction mass, partially obstructing, ulcerated and friable — suspicious for malignancy. Biopsies obtained, pathology pending. Mild antral gastritis. Recommend staging (EUS, cross-sectional imaging) and multidisciplinary referral.

findings.finding: Partially circumferential ulcerated friable mass, luminal narrowing, suspicious for malignancy

procedure.indication: Positive fecal immunochemical test (FIT) — diagnostic colonoscopy
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol) with anesthesiology present; selected over endoscopist-directed moderate sedation given HFrEF EF 25% and ICD. ICD managed per EP plan (magnet on standby, no reprogramming required for lower endoscopy). Continuous hemodynamic and telemetry monitoring.
