procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with polypectomy. INDICATION: positive FIT (2026-06-28) in a 58-year-old woman with ischemic cardiomyopathy, HFrEF (EF 25%), single-chamber ICD, and new-onset atrial fibrillation. ANTICOAGULATION: apixaban 5 mg BID held for 3 days pre-procedure per plan coordinated with cardiology/EP (last dose morning of 2026-08-03); low-dose aspirin 81 mg continued through the procedure. SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care with anesthesiology present given cardiac risk; ICD managed per EP plan. PREP: adequate (BBPS 7). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. A 9 mm pedunculated polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by snare polypectomy; a prophylactic hemoclip was placed on the stalk given interrupted anticoagulation. A 6 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare. Two diminutive (2-3 mm) polyps in the descending colon were removed by cold biopsy forceps. Non-bleeding sigmoid diverticulosis was noted without stigmata of recent hemorrhage. Internal hemorrhoids, small, non-bleeding. No mass, no ulceration, and no active bleeding identified. All specimens retrieved and sent to pathology. IMPRESSION: Multiple colon polyps removed (ascending 9 mm pedunculated, sigmoid 6 mm, two descending diminutive); diverticulosis; internal hemorrhoids. Positive FIT most likely attributable to the polyps; no other bleeding source identified. Pathology pending. Given interrupted therapeutic anticoagulation and clip placement, delayed post-polypectomy bleeding risk counseled.

procedure.indication: Iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 9.6, ferritin 11) with new melena — evaluate for GI bleeding source
procedure.sedation: General anesthesia with endotracheal intubation, anesthesiologist-directed. Difficult-airway precautions: awake video laryngoscopy (GlideScope) with bougie, given documented Cormack-Lehane grade 3 (2022), untreated severe OSA, and class III obesity (BMI 54). Weekly semaglutide held 3 weeks pre-procedure; extended clear-liquid diet and prolonged fast; point-of-care gastric ultrasound day-of showed empty antrum. Airway secured on first attempt with video laryngoscopy.
procedure.prep_quality: Colonoscopy: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 (right 2, transverse 2, left 3) — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: EGD: second/third portion of duodenum. Colonoscopy: cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated.

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: EGD followed by colonoscopy under general anesthesia with difficult-airway precautions. INDICATION: iron-deficiency anemia with new melena. EGD FINDINGS: The esophagus was normal with a normal Z-line; no varices. In the gastric antrum a single 15 mm clean-based to fibrin-covered ulcer was identified along the lesser curvature with an adherent clot; after clot washout a non-bleeding visible vessel (Forrest IIa) was seen. Hemostasis achieved with epinephrine injection (1:10,000) and placement of two through-the-scope hemoclips; no active bleeding on completion. The ulcer margins were irregular and were biopsied (4 samples) to exclude malignancy. Gastric body/antral mucosal biopsies obtained for H. pylori. The duodenal bulb and second/third portions were normal. COLONOSCOPY FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum; terminal ileum normal. A single 6 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon was removed by cold snare and retrieved. Scattered non-bleeding diverticula in the sigmoid. No colonic source of bleeding or angioectasia identified. IMPRESSION: 15 mm antral gastric ulcer with non-bleeding visible vessel (Forrest IIa) — endoscopic hemostasis performed; ulcer biopsied to exclude malignancy. H. pylori testing pending. 6 mm ascending colon polyp removed. Diverticulosis. Melena attributed to the gastric ulcer. Pathology pending.

findings.action_taken: epinephrine injection + 2 hemoclips (hemostasis achieved); margin biopsies x4 to exclude malignancy

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with cold-forceps biopsies. INDICATION: iron-deficiency anemia with positive FOBT and weight loss in an 88-year-old; occult GI blood loss/malignancy workup. CONSENT: informed consent obtained from healthcare POA (daughter) as surrogate given patient's moderate dementia; capacity for independent consent not established. ANTICOAGULATION: apixaban (patient confirmed on 5 mg PO BID per pharmacy bottle; chart had listed 2.5 mg BID — reconciled) held for 48 hours pre-procedure in coordination with cardiology; last dose morning of 2026-08-04. SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care (propofol). PREP: adequate but suboptimal in the right colon (BBPS 6). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with terminal ileum intubation. In the ascending colon a partially circumferential, fungating, friable mass approximately 4 cm was identified, occupying roughly one-third to one-half of the luminal circumference, non-obstructing, with contact bleeding on gentle manipulation and no active spontaneous bleeding. The lumen remained traversable. Multiple cold-forceps biopsies (6) were obtained from the mass margin and sent to pathology (specimen A); the site was tattooed with sterile carbon ink distally for future localization. A separate 6 mm sessile polyp was noted in the sigmoid colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy (specimen B). Scattered non-bleeding diverticula were present in the sigmoid. The terminal ileum and the remainder of the examined colon were otherwise unremarkable. IMPRESSION: Fungating ~4 cm mass in the ascending colon, suspicious for malignancy — biopsied and tattooed, pathology pending; likely source of the iron-deficiency anemia and occult blood loss. 6 mm sigmoid polyp removed. Diverticulosis. PLAN: Expedite pathology; staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and CEA; multidisciplinary review with colorectal surgery and oncology in the context of goals of care and the patient's frailty/dementia; communicate results with POA.

findings.finding: Fungating partially circumferential friable mass, ~4 cm, non-obstructing, contact bleeding — suspicious for malignancy
findings.action_taken: 6 cold-forceps biopsies obtained; site tattooed distally

findings.finding: 6 mm sessile polyp
Diverticulosis, no stigmata of recent bleeding

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)
procedure.indication: Persistent GERD despite daily PPI (omeprazole 20 mg)
procedure.sedation: Moderate (conscious) sedation — midazolam 3 mg IV, fentanyl 100 mcg IV. Propofol avoided pending/per Anesthesiology adjudication of unconfirmed 2021 reaction; Anesthesiology reviewed the 2021 pre-anesthesia record and recommended a non-propofol sedation plan for this exam. Mild intermittent asthma noted; no periprocedural bronchospasm, SpO2 maintained 96-99% on 2 L NC.
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy) — NPO >8 hours, adequate; clear gastric field
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum (D2) intubated; complete diagnostic exam of esophagus, stomach, and duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsies. INDICATION: persistent GERD despite omeprazole 20 mg daily. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl); propofol avoided per Anesthesiology adjudication of unconfirmed 2021 reaction. FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed under direct vision into the esophagus. The gastroesophageal junction was located at 40 cm; a small (approximately 2 cm) sliding hiatal hernia was present. In the distal esophagus there were several linear mucosal breaks, some confluent between the tops of adjacent folds — consistent with LA Grade B erosive esophagitis. No circumferential ulceration, no stricture, and no obvious salmon-colored columnar mucosa/tongue suggestive of Barrett's; no mass or luminal narrowing. The stomach was examined in forward and retroflexed views: mild antral erythema consistent with gastritis, no ulcer, no mass; retained mucus cleared without difficulty. Gastric antral and body biopsies obtained for Helicobacter pylori. The pylorus was normal and traversed; the duodenal bulb and second portion were normal without ulcer or mass; duodenal biopsies obtained to evaluate for celiac disease given the biopsy-during-exam protocol. Distal esophageal biopsies obtained to exclude eosinophilic esophagitis and to characterize inflammation. No active bleeding at any point. IMPRESSION: (1) LA Grade B erosive esophagitis. (2) Small sliding hiatal hernia. (3) Mild gastritis; H. pylori biopsies obtained. Pathology pending.

findings.action_taken: biopsies obtained (rule out eosinophilic esophagitis / characterize inflammation)

findings.finding: Small (~2 cm) sliding hiatal hernia
Mild gastritis (antral erythema), no ulcer or mass
Normal duodenal bulb and second portion, no ulcer/mass
No Barrett's esophagus, no stricture, no mass, no active bleeding

procedure.name: Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM)
procedure.indication: Achalasia, Chicago classification type II, refractory to pneumatic dilation, with ongoing nutritional decline (~20 lb loss, BMI 18.4, albumin 3.3)
procedure.sedation: General endotracheal anesthesia (rapid-sequence induction given retained esophageal contents/aspiration risk); CO2 insufflation throughout
procedure.prep_quality: Extended clear-liquid diet x48h; residual liquid/food debris in dilated esophagus lavaged and suctioned prior to myotomy — acceptable working field
procedure.extent_reached: Esophagus, gastroesophageal junction, and proximal gastric cardia; submucosal tunnel extended ~3 cm onto the gastric side

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM). INDICATION: achalasia type II refractory to pneumatic dilation with nutritional decline. ANESTHESIA: general endotracheal with rapid-sequence induction; CO2 insufflation. PREP: extended clear-liquid diet; dilated esophagus contained residual liquid and food debris which was lavaged and suctioned to obtain an acceptable field. FINDINGS: Dilated esophageal body with a tight, non-relaxing gastroesophageal junction consistent with achalasia; no mucosal mass or suspicious lesion; no evidence of Candida esophagitis. Given prior LES botulinum toxin injection (~2 years ago) and prior pneumatic dilation, submucosal fibrosis was anticipated and encountered. TECHNIQUE: A mucosotomy was created on the posterior wall of the mid-esophagus at ~28 cm. A submucosal tunnel was dissected distally across the GEJ onto the gastric cardia. Submucosal fibrosis at the GEJ increased dissection difficulty and prolonged tunneling but the tunnel was completed. A selective (inner circular) myotomy was performed beginning ~10 cm above the GEJ and extended ~3 cm onto the gastric side (total myotomy length ~13 cm). Improved distensibility of the GEJ was noted after myotomy. The mucosotomy entry was closed with endoscopic clips without difficulty. No mucosal perforation of the tunnel was identified. No capnoperitoneum requiring decompression. Estimated blood loss minimal. IMPRESSION: Technically successful POEM for achalasia type II; increased technical difficulty due to post-botox/post-dilation submucosal fibrosis. No intraprocedural mucosal injury or perforation.

findings.action_taken: posterior selective myotomy ~13 cm (10 cm esophageal + 3 cm gastric)

procedure.name: Endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD), gastric antrum
procedure.indication: Gastric antral adenoma with biopsy-proven high-grade dysplasia; EUS without submucosal invasion — en-bloc endoscopic resection
procedure.sedation: General anesthesia with endotracheal intubation for airway protection
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper GI); stomach adequately emptied after overnight fast
procedure.extent_reached: Gastric antrum lesion resected; scope advanced to second portion of duodenum, remainder of stomach and esophagus surveyed

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) of gastric antral lesion. INDICATION: 25 mm flat, slightly depressed gastric antral lesion with biopsy-proven high-grade dysplasia; staging EUS without submucosal invasion or perigastric lymphadenopathy. ANTITHROMBOTIC HOLD: Low-dose aspirin (primary prevention) held per high-bleed-risk resection protocol without cardiology clearance; OTC fish oil (omega-3) and OTC ibuprofen held pre-procedure; amlodipine continued through morning of procedure. Pre-procedure labs acceptable (Hgb 13.9, plt 244, INR 1.0). SEDATION: general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation. FINDINGS: The esophagus and gastroesophageal junction were normal. In the gastric antrum, the previously marked 25 mm flat, slightly depressed lesion with abnormal narrow-band-imaging surface pattern was identified. Circumferential marking dots were placed 5 mm outside the lesion margin. Submucosal injection of a lifting solution with indigo carmine produced a good lift with no non-lifting sign. Circumferential mucosal incision followed by submucosal dissection was performed, achieving en-bloc resection of the lesion on a single specimen. The resection bed was inspected; several visible vessels were coagulated prophylactically with coagulation forceps. No perforation. No active bleeding at completion. The specimen was retrieved, pinned, and sent to pathology for en-bloc margin assessment. The remainder of the stomach and the examined duodenum were normal. IMPRESSION: Successful en-bloc ESD of gastric antral high-grade dysplasia lesion; no immediate complication. Pathology pending for depth of invasion and lateral/deep margin status.

findings.finding: 25 mm flat, slightly depressed antral lesion with abnormal NBI surface pattern, resected en-bloc
findings.action_taken: endoscopic submucosal dissection, en-bloc, specimen retrieved

findings.finding: Resection bed with visible vessels, no active bleeding
Normal esophagus, remaining stomach, and examined duodenum

Add amlodipine 5 mg daily to reconciled medication list; continue for hypertension

procedure.name: Esophageal dilation (serial #4, Savary/wire-guided)
procedure.indication: Recurrent solid-food dysphagia from radiation-induced proximal-to-mid esophageal stricture; serial dilation program (#4)
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (MAC) with propofol; anesthesia team advised of codeine intolerance
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy; NPO >8 hours confirmed, aspiration precautions observed given post-radiation upper airway anatomy)
procedure.extent_reached: Stomach and duodenum (second portion) after stricture traversal; full upper endoscopic survey completed

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with wire-guided (Savary) esophageal dilation, serial session #4. INDICATION: recurrent solid-food dysphagia with ~4 lb interval weight loss; known radiation-induced proximal-to-mid esophageal stricture s/p chemoradiation for laryngeal carcinoma (in remission). SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care (propofol); difficult post-radiation upper airway anatomy managed without event. ANTITHROMBOTICS: none on board (OTC fish oil is not a therapeutic anticoagulant; no hold required, none held). FINDINGS: The upper esophagus demonstrated expected post-radiation mucosal changes. A focal, benign-appearing fibrotic stricture was identified in the proximal-to-mid esophagus with an estimated pre-dilation luminal diameter of ~11-12 mm, restenosed from the 15 mm achieved at session #3. The stricture was non-traversable with the adult gastroscope on first pass. A guidewire was passed under fluoroscopic guidance and the stricture was dilated in graded fashion with Savary bougies to 16 mm; moderate resistance encountered, with a satisfactory dilation effect and only minor mucosal tearing, no significant bleeding. There was no perforation. Post-dilation the scope traversed the stricture and a complete survey of the distal esophagus, stomach, and duodenum to the second portion was normal. No mass, no ulcer, no active bleeding. Surveillance biopsies were taken from the stricture to exclude recurrent/second malignancy given the laryngeal carcinoma and radiation history. IMPRESSION: Benign-appearing recurrent radiation esophageal stricture successfully dilated to 16 mm (graded Savary); technically successful, no perforation. Surveillance biopsies pending. Continue serial dilation program.

findings.finding: Recurrent benign-appearing fibrotic esophageal stricture, ~11-12 mm pre-dilation (restenosis from prior 15 mm)
findings.action_taken: wire-guided graded Savary dilation to 16 mm; surveillance biopsies obtained

procedure.name: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) placement
procedure.indication: ALS with progressive dysphagia, inadequate oral intake and weight loss; enteral access. Expedited given declining respiratory reserve (FVC 43% predicted).
procedure.sedation: Minimized MAC with anesthesia present; low-dose propofol titrated, topical pharyngeal lidocaine, no benzodiazepine. Non-supine (head-of-bed elevated ~30 degrees) positioning. Nocturnal NIV equipment and respiratory therapy available at bedside.
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy) — NPO confirmed; OTC fish oil held pre-procedure per instruction, no prescription anticoagulant to hold.
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; gastrostomy sited on anterior gastric body

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) placement via pull technique. INDICATION: ALS with progressive dysphagia, weight loss, and inadequate oral intake; enteral access expedited due to declining respiratory reserve. SEDATION: minimized anesthesia-supported (MAC) with head-of-bed elevated, non-supine positioning; respiratory therapy and NIV available. PROPHYLAXIS: single dose IV cefazolin given pre-procedure. FINDINGS: The gastroscope was passed under direct vision through the oropharynx and advanced to the second portion of the duodenum. Esophagus without stricture or obstructing lesion; some pooled secretions consistent with impaired clearance. Stomach normal without ulcer, mass, or retained food. Adequate transillumination and single-finger indentation confirmed on the anterior gastric body. Under sterile technique the site was infiltrated with local anesthetic, a needle/cannula was passed into the stomach under endoscopic visualization, a guidewire was snared and withdrawn transorally, and a 20 Fr PEG tube was placed by the pull technique. Internal bumper seen well-apposed to the gastric wall on repeat endoscopy; external bolster set at 3 cm with a small gap. No bleeding at the puncture site. Tolerated the procedure without desaturation; SpO2 maintained 94-96%. IMPRESSION: Successful 20 Fr PEG placement, technically uncomplicated. No obstructing esophageal or gastric lesion. Pooled esophageal secretions consistent with bulbar dysphagia.

findings.finding: 20 Fr PEG tube placed by pull technique, anterior gastric body; internal bumper well-apposed, external bolster at 3 cm
Pooled esophageal/pharyngeal secretions consistent with bulbar dysphagia; no stricture or obstructing lesion
Normal stomach and duodenum; no ulcer, mass, or bleeding

procedure.name: Colonoscopy
procedure.indication: Active hematochezia in an anticoagulated patient (rivaroxaban) with orthostasis and anemia (Hgb 9.6); locate and treat lower GI bleeding source. Performed after ED stabilization, volume resuscitation, and coordinated hold of rivaroxaban with cardiology.

procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 6/9 — adequate (urgent inpatient prep; some residual liquid stool in right colon cleared with irrigation)
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; terminal ileum intubated)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with endoscopic hemostasis. INDICATION: active hematochezia on rivaroxaban with orthostasis and anemia. Rivaroxaban held pre-procedure per cardiology; patient volume-resuscitated in ED with stabilized hemodynamics prior to scope. SEDATION: MAC/propofol, anesthesia-directed. PREP: adequate after irrigation (BBPS 6). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated with no blood or lesion in the ileum, excluding a more proximal source. Moderate diverticulosis was present throughout the sigmoid and descending colon. In the proximal sigmoid, a single diverticulum demonstrated a non-bleeding visible vessel with adherent clot and surrounding fresh blood — identified as the culprit diverticular bleeding source. The clot was gently irrigated; hemostasis was achieved with placement of two through-the-scope hemoclips across the vessel, with no further bleeding after treatment. No mass, no ulcer, and no stigmata of active bleeding elsewhere. Non-bleeding internal hemorrhoids noted on retroflexion in the rectum. IMPRESSION: Diverticular hemorrhage from a proximal sigmoid diverticulum, treated successfully with hemoclips; diverticulosis; non-bleeding internal hemorrhoids. No malignancy identified.

findings.finding: Diverticular bleeding source — non-bleeding visible vessel with adherent clot and fresh blood
findings.action_taken: clot irrigation; endoscopic hemostasis with 2 through-the-scope hemoclips; hemostasis achieved

findings.finding: Moderate diverticulosis, no other stigmata of bleeding
Non-bleeding internal hemorrhoids
Normal terminal ileum — no blood, excludes proximal source

contacts.rationale: Triage trap — a med-timing error (patient restarted anticoagulation before the sanctioned date) plus recurrent hematochezia and orthostasis is a red-flag rebleed on a DOAC requiring emergent action, not reassurance.

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with Barrett's surveillance biopsies
procedure.indication: Surveillance of Barrett's esophagus (C2M4, no prior dysplasia); asymptomatic, scheduled surveillance due
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 3 mg, fentanyl 100 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy); patient NPO >8 hours, adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with Barrett's surveillance biopsies. INDICATION: Surveillance of known Barrett's esophagus (C2M4, no dysplasia on 2024 biopsies); patient asymptomatic. ANTICOAGULATION: Patient on apixaban 5 mg BID; last dose held 2 days pre-procedure per periprocedural plan (low thrombotic risk per hematology, no bridging). SEDATION: Moderate sedation, well tolerated. FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed under direct vision to the second portion of the duodenum. ESOPHAGUS: Salmon-colored columnar-lined mucosa extending in a tongue-like fashion in the distal esophagus, Prague classification C2M4, unchanged from prior. No visible nodularity, ulceration, or mucosal irregularity. No suspicious lesion for targeted biopsy. Four-quadrant biopsies obtained every 2 cm per Seattle protocol. Z-line at 38 cm, diaphragmatic hiatus at 40 cm (small hiatal hernia). STOMACH: Normal mucosa, no ulcer or mass; retroflexion normal. DUODENUM: Normal bulb and second portion. No active bleeding at any point. IMPRESSION: Barrett's esophagus C2M4, no visible dysplasia or nodularity, stable appearance. Small hiatal hernia. Seattle-protocol surveillance biopsies obtained; pathology pending. No complications.

Small hiatal hernia
Normal stomach and duodenum

procedure.name: Colonoscopy with endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR)
procedure.indication: Surveillance colonoscopy (prior sigmoid adenoma); large laterally spreading sigmoid lesion identified for resection
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesiologist-directed
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8/9 — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve intubation)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with piecemeal endoscopic mucosal resection. INDICATION: surveillance colonoscopy with a large laterally spreading sigmoid lesion identified for resection; history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on apixaban (held 48h pre-procedure per anticoagulation plan) and hypertension. SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care (propofol). PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum. In the sigmoid colon a single 22 mm laterally spreading, sessile/granular lesion (Paris 0-IIa + Is) was identified. Submucosal injection of lifted saline/methylene blue solution produced a good lift with no non-lifting sign. The lesion was removed by piecemeal endoscopic mucosal resection using a stiff snare; all visible tissue was resected and specimens retrieved for histology. The post-resection defect was inspected: several non-bleeding visible vessels were noted at the margin and base and were treated prophylactically with through-the-scope clips (5 clips placed). No immediate intraprocedural bleeding at completion. Scattered sigmoid diverticula without stigmata of bleeding. The remainder of the examined colon and terminal ileum were normal. IMPRESSION: 22 mm sigmoid laterally spreading lesion removed by piecemeal EMR; prophylactic clip closure of the resection base. Histology pending. Delayed post-polypectomy bleeding precautions given; apixaban to resume post-procedure Day 2 (2026-07-16) per plan.

findings.finding: 22 mm laterally spreading sessile granular lesion (Paris 0-IIa+Is)
findings.action_taken: piecemeal endoscopic mucosal resection after submucosal lift; specimens retrieved

findings.finding: Post-EMR mucosal defect with non-bleeding visible vessels
Diverticulosis, no stigmata of bleeding

pathology.report: Sigmoid colon, piecemeal EMR: tubulovillous adenoma with focal high-grade dysplasia. No definite submucosal invasion identified in the fragments examined; deep and lateral margins cannot be assessed due to the piecemeal nature of the resection. No high-risk features of invasive carcinoma (no lymphovascular invasion, no poor differentiation) seen in submitted material.
pathology.diagnosis: Tubulovillous adenoma with focal high-grade dysplasia (piecemeal EMR, margins not assessable)

procedure.name: ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy and stone extraction
procedure.indication: Choledocholithiasis — 7 mm distal CBD stone on MRCP with mild upstream ductal dilatation and transient jaundice
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesiology-directed
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy / ERCP)
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; major papilla identified and cannulated

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy and stone extraction. INDICATION: choledocholithiasis (7 mm distal CBD stone on MRCP). SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care (propofol). FINDINGS: The duodenoscope was advanced to the second portion of the duodenum. The major papilla was identified and appeared normal. Selective deep cannulation of the common bile duct was achieved using a sphincterotome and guidewire; the pancreatic duct was not deeply instrumented. Occlusion cholangiogram demonstrated a single 7 mm filling defect in the distal CBD with mild upstream ductal dilatation, consistent with the known stone. A biliary sphincterotomy was performed with good bile flow. Balloon sweep of the duct extracted one stone; a repeat occlusion cholangiogram showed clearance with no residual filling defect and free flow of contrast. No biliary stent was placed. Estimated blood loss minimal. No immediate procedural complication. IMPRESSION: Successful biliary sphincterotomy with extraction of a single distal CBD stone and complete duct clearance. Patient not on antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy. Gallbladder in situ with cholelithiasis — outpatient cholecystectomy referral to follow after biliary clearance.

findings.finding: Single 7 mm filling defect (stone) in distal common bile duct with mild upstream ductal dilatation
findings.action_taken: biliary sphincterotomy + balloon sweep extraction; duct cleared

findings.finding: Normal major papilla
Complete duct clearance on repeat occlusion cholangiogram, no residual defect

complications.detail: New epigastric pain radiating to back with one episode of emesis on post-op day 1. STAT lipase + CBC ordered; diagnosis suspected, not yet confirmed pending enzyme results. Managed as urgent escalation.

If lipase >3x ULN or clinical deterioration, arrange same-day IV crystalloid resuscitation and admission consideration

Correct visit disposition in chart from routine follow-up to urgent post-procedure evaluation
Outpatient cholecystectomy referral after biliary clearance (gallbladder in situ with cholelithiasis)

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with balloon dilation of esophageal stricture

code.display: Endoscopic balloon dilatation of stricture of esophagus (procedure)

procedure.indication: Progressive solid-food dysphagia with suspected peptic (reflux-related) distal esophageal stricture; long-standing GERD
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol)
procedure.prep_quality: NPO per protocol; adequate for upper endoscopy
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with through-the-scope balloon dilation. INDICATION: progressive solid-food dysphagia, suspected peptic esophageal stricture, long-standing GERD. SEDATION: monitored anesthesia care (propofol). FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed without difficulty into the esophagus. A smooth, tapered stricture was identified in the distal esophagus at approximately 36 cm, appearance consistent with a reflux-related (peptic) stricture; no malignant-appearing tissue, mass, or ulceration. LA Grade A esophagitis noted in the distal esophagus. Targeted biopsies were obtained from the stricture and distal esophagus. Through-the-scope balloon dilation was performed to 15 mm with a good luminal result; the scope traversed the segment easily after dilation. No immediate bleeding, mucosal tear beyond expected dilation effect, or perforation. The stomach was examined in forward and retroflexed views and was unremarkable; no significant hiatal hernia. The pylorus, duodenal bulb, and second portion of the duodenum were normal. No active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Distal peptic esophageal stricture successfully dilated to 15 mm; LA Grade A esophagitis; biopsies obtained. Technical success. Pathology pending.

findings.finding: Smooth tapered peptic (reflux-related) esophageal stricture
findings.action_taken: through-the-scope balloon dilation to 15 mm with good luminal result

findings.finding: LA Grade A esophagitis
Normal stomach, duodenal bulb, and second portion of duodenum

pathology.report: Distal esophageal biopsies: reflux-associated (peptic) changes; no dysplasia, no malignancy, no eosinophilic infiltrate (eosinophil count within normal limits). Final report pending confirmation.
pathology.diagnosis: Reflux-associated esophageal mucosal changes; no dysplasia or malignancy

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Tolerated clear liquids in recovery. Soft diet x48h then advance as tolerated. Resume home medications including pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily; resume amlodipine. Return precautions (fever, chest pain, inability to swallow liquids, hematemesis, melena) reviewed. Routine GI clinic follow-up.

Anticipate likely need for repeat endoscopic dilation for recurrent/refractory peptic stricture; re-dilate if dysphagia recurs

procedure.name: Colonoscopy
procedure.indication: Average-risk colorectal cancer screening (72M, screening overdue)
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8/9 — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: average-risk colorectal cancer screening. SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl). PREP: adequate (BBPS 8/9). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum, confirmed by the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. Two diminutive (3-4 mm) sessile polyps were identified, one in the ascending colon and one in the sigmoid colon; both were removed en bloc by cold snare polypectomy and fully retrieved (specimens A and B). The remainder of the examined colon and rectum (including retroflexion in the rectum) were normal, with no masses, no significant diverticulosis, and no source of bleeding. No active bleeding at the polypectomy sites at completion. The patient is not on any prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet agent; his daily fish oil (omega-3) is an over-the-counter supplement and carried no bleeding-risk requirement. IMPRESSION: Two diminutive polyps (ascending and sigmoid) removed by cold snare; otherwise normal screening colonoscopy to the cecum. Pathology pending.

findings.finding: Diminutive (3-4 mm) sessile polyp
Diminutive (3-4 mm) sessile polyp
Normal terminal ileum, colon, and rectum otherwise; no mass or bleeding

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort (wife). Post-polypectomy precautions reviewed; tolerated procedure well, no immediate bleeding or pain.

followup.recommendation: Review polyp pathology (specimens A and B); call patient with result and set surveillance colonoscopy interval accordingly
Set colonoscopy surveillance interval — likely extended (e.g., 7-10 years) if only 1-2 small tubular adenomas or hyperplastic polyps on final pathology
No anticoagulant hold/resume required — patient's 'blood thinner' is OTC fish oil (omega-3), not a prescription anticoagulant; may resume anytime
Continue lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension; BP at goal

procedure.name: Flexible sigmoidoscopy (limited, unprepped)
procedure.indication: Acute severe ulcerative colitis flare — endoscopic severity grading and mucosal biopsies including CMV immunostains. Referral requested full colonoscopy; amended to limited unprepped flexible sigmoidoscopy given elevated perforation/decompensation risk in acute severe colitis.
procedure.sedation: None (unsedated limited exam; patient tolerated well)
procedure.prep_quality: Unprepped (no bowel preparation given acute severe flare) — mucosa adequately visualized in the rectosigmoid/left colon; luminal contents present but did not preclude severity grading
procedure.extent_reached: Descending colon / proximal to splenic flexure (deliberately limited exam; scope not advanced further to minimize perforation risk)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Limited unprepped flexible sigmoidoscopy. INDICATION: acute severe ulcerative colitis flare for severity grading and biopsies (including CMV). SEDATION: none. PREP: unprepped. FINDINGS: The sigmoidoscope was advanced carefully to the descending colon, just short of the splenic flexure; the exam was deliberately limited to reduce perforation risk in the setting of acute severe colitis. The examined mucosa from the rectum through the distal descending colon demonstrated continuous, confluent severe inflammation with loss of vascular pattern, marked friability with spontaneous bleeding, and deep ulceration — Mayo endoscopic subscore 3. No discrete mass, stricture, or large-caliber vessel with active arterial bleeding was seen; oozing was diffuse from friable ulcerated mucosa. Cold-forceps biopsies were obtained from the sigmoid and descending colon for histology and CMV immunohistochemistry/PCR. The scope was withdrawn. No immediate complication. IMPRESSION: Continuous severe left-sided colitis, Mayo endoscopic subscore 3, with deep ulceration consistent with acute severe ulcerative colitis; biopsies including CMV immunostains pending. Findings support admission for IV corticosteroids with early consideration of rescue therapy if inadequate response.

findings.finding: Continuous severe colitis with loss of vascular pattern, marked friability, spontaneous bleeding, and deep ulceration (Mayo endoscopic subscore 3)
Diffuse mucosal oozing from friable ulcerated mucosa, no discrete bleeding vessel
No mass, stricture, polyp, or discrete lesion identified

disposition: Admitted to inpatient GI/IBD service directly from endoscopy. Plan for IV methylprednisolone, VTE prophylaxis, daily monitoring of stool frequency/CRP/albumin, and surgical + IBD co-management. Rescue therapy (infliximab or cyclosporine) to be considered on day 3 if inadequate response to IV steroids.

followup.recommendation: Review CMV immunostain/PCR and histology on colonic biopsies; if CMV positive, initiate antiviral therapy (e.g., ganciclovir) — critical given concurrent corticosteroids
Admit for IV corticosteroids and monitor response; assess need for rescue therapy at day 3
Surgical consultation (colorectal) for co-management given acute severe colitis and colectomy contingency if refractory
Monitor anemia (Hgb 10.9) and hemodynamics; transfuse per threshold and reassess iron studies during admission
Reinforce mesalamine adherence (adherence gap preceded flare) and continue prednisone; reconcile medication list to reflect prednisone 40 mg daily

procedure.name: Colonoscopy
procedure.indication: Surveillance colonoscopy — history of tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia (2021); due 2026. No GI symptoms, no alarm features.
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8/9 (right 3, transverse 3, left 2) — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with polypectomy. INDICATION: surveillance for prior tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia (2021). SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl). PREP: adequate (BBPS 8). ANTIPLATELET MANAGEMENT: Clopidogrel and patient-disclosed daily OTC aspirin 81 mg were held periprocedurally per protocol (PAD, no stents — hold confirmed safe); fish oil supplement paused. Certified Cantonese interpreter used for day-of consent (interpreter ID documented); family interpretation not used. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. A 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved (Specimen A). A 3 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy; specimen retrieved (Specimen B). Polypectomy sites were inspected with no immediate bleeding; given periprocedural antiplatelet status, sites were examined carefully and a prophylactic clip was placed at the ascending polypectomy site. Scattered sigmoid diverticulosis without stigmata of bleeding. The remainder of the examined colon was normal. No active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Two small polyps removed (5 mm ascending, 3 mm sigmoid); diverticulosis. Pathology pending. Careful hemostasis given held dual antiplatelet therapy.

findings.action_taken: cold snare polypectomy, retrieved; prophylactic clip placed

procedure.name: ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction (fluoro-minimization protocol in pregnancy)
procedure.indication: Symptomatic choledocholithiasis with rising cholestatic LFTs and features concerning for early ascending cholangitis, at 24 weeks gestation
procedure.sedation: General endotracheal anesthesia with OB-experienced anesthesia team; MFM present, continuous fetal heart rate monitoring pre- and post-procedure
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (ERCP)
procedure.extent_reached: Major papilla cannulated; common bile duct selectively accessed

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy and stone extraction. INDICATION: symptomatic choledocholithiasis with rising LFTs and overnight rigors/subjective fever concerning for early ascending cholangitis, at 24 weeks gestation. Case expedited from routine queue to urgent tier after referral review. ANESTHESIA: general endotracheal, OB-experienced anesthesia; MFM coordinated peri-procedure with continuous fetal heart rate monitoring; patient positioned with left lateral tilt to avoid aortocaval compression; abdominal/pelvic lead shielding applied. RADIATION MINIMIZATION: fluoro-minimization protocol used — bile duct cannulation confirmed by bile aspiration and wire-guided technique; a single brief low-dose spot fluoroscopy image obtained (total fluoro time <10 seconds) with collimation. CONTRAST: given prior iodinated-contrast reaction (pruritus/hives), corticosteroid + antihistamine premedication administered per allergy protocol and contrast injection minimized (diluted, small-volume cholangiogram). ANTIPLATELET: low-dose aspirin 81 mg (preeclampsia prophylaxis) continued per plan — not held for sphincterotomy. FINDINGS: The major papilla was identified and selectively cannulated. Cholangiogram demonstrated a dilated common bile duct (~8 mm) with a single filling defect in the distal CBD consistent with an obstructing stone; purulent/cloudy bile expressed on access, supporting early cholangitis. A biliary sphincterotomy was performed; a single ~6 mm stone was extracted with a balloon sweep with clearance of the duct and brisk bile drainage. Occlusion balloon sweep confirmed no residual filling defect. Given cholangitis physiology, a 7 Fr x 7 cm plastic biliary stent was placed to ensure ongoing drainage. Hemostasis at the sphincterotomy site was adequate with no active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Obstructing distal CBD stone extracted; biliary sphincterotomy performed; plastic biliary stent placed for early cholangitis. Duct clearance confirmed. No immediate complication; fetal heart tones reassuring post-procedure.

findings.finding: Dilated common bile duct (~8 mm) with single distal obstructing stone
findings.action_taken: biliary sphincterotomy and balloon extraction; duct cleared

findings.finding: Cloudy/purulent bile on access, consistent with early ascending cholangitis
findings.action_taken: bile drainage; blood cultures and empiric antibiotics per inpatient team

procedure.name: Colonoscopy with ileal intubation and biopsies
procedure.indication: Suspected new-onset inflammatory bowel disease (fistulizing phenotype); chronic bloody diarrhea, weight loss, perianal fistula, fecal calprotectin 890 ug/g — diagnostic colonoscopy to confirm diagnosis and obtain tissue
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 4 mg, fentanyl 100 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 7/9 — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Terminal ileum (successful ileal intubation via ileocecal valve, ~15 cm ileum examined)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy with ileal intubation and segmental biopsies. INDICATION: suspected new-onset inflammatory bowel disease, fistulizing phenotype; confirm diagnosis and map disease. SEDATION: moderate. PREP: adequate (BBPS 7). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum and the terminal ileum was intubated. TERMINAL ILEUM: multiple aphthous and larger linear ulcers with surrounding erythema and edema over ~15 cm of examined ileum; biopsies obtained. RIGHT/TRANSVERSE COLON: patchy erythema, loss of vascular pattern, scattered aphthous ulcers with intervening normal-appearing mucosa (skip lesions); biopsies obtained. LEFT COLON/SIGMOID: focal cobblestoning and a deep serpiginous ulcer, moderate friability; biopsies obtained. RECTUM: relative rectal sparing with mild patchy inflammation; biopsies obtained. PERIANAL/ANORECTAL: on retroflexion and external inspection, an external perianal opening with a fistulous tract and a skin tag were noted, consistent with perianal fistulizing disease. No active brisk bleeding; contact friability only. No large mass or stricture precluding passage; the scope traversed all segments. IMPRESSION: Discontinuous (skip) ileocolonic ulceration with cobblestoning, relative rectal sparing, and perianal fistulizing disease — endoscopic appearance highly consistent with Crohn's disease. Segmental biopsies obtained throughout for histologic confirmation. Correlate with histology and MR enterography.

Cobblestoning and deep serpiginous ulcer, moderate friability
Relative rectal sparing with mild patchy inflammation
External perianal fistulous opening with skin tag (perianal fistulizing disease)
findings.action_taken: documented; anatomy to be further characterized on MR enterography/pelvis

procedure.indication: Colorectal cancer screening — prior incomplete exam (2018, inadequate prep); first-degree family history of CRC (father, ~70s)
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 3 mg, fentanyl 100 mcg)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: colorectal cancer screening; prior 2018 attempt aborted for inadequate preparation; newly reported first-degree family history of colon cancer (father, early 70s). PREP: This time a low-volume split-dose PEG-based preparation was used with pre-procedure ondansetron given the documented 2018 tolerability issue (nausea/vomiting with high-volume PEG — an intolerance, not a true allergy). Patient tolerated the prep without vomiting. Bowel preparation was adequate overall (BBPS 7/9). SEDATION: moderate (midazolam/fentanyl), well tolerated. FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum, confirmed by identification of the appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve; the terminal ileum was intubated and normal. Unlike the 2018 aborted exam, a complete diagnostic examination was achieved. A 5 mm sessile polyp was identified in the ascending colon and removed by cold snare polypectomy (specimen A, retrieved). A 4 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon was removed by cold snare polypectomy (specimen B, retrieved). Scattered non-complicated diverticula were noted in the sigmoid colon without stigmata of bleeding. Findings consistent with the patient's baseline constipation; no mass, ulceration, stricture, or luminal narrowing. No active bleeding. IMPRESSION: Complete screening colonoscopy to cecum with adequate prep. Two diminutive polyps removed (ascending, sigmoid). Sigmoid diverticulosis. Pathology pending. No alarm features and no concerning mucosal lesion identified.

procedure.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with hemostasis
procedure.indication: Suspected upper GI bleeding (collateral history of two melena episodes) on chronic self-directed NSAID (naproxen) use; GERD
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesiologist-directed
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy); NPO >8 hours, adequate mucosal visualization
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of the duodenum (D2)

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with endoscopic hemostasis. INDICATION: Suspected upper GI bleeding (collateral melena x2 last week) in the setting of chronic daily naproxen use and GERD; naproxen held since clinic visit. SEDATION: MAC/propofol. FINDINGS: The endoscope was passed without difficulty to the second portion of the duodenum. ESOPHAGUS: Los Angeles Grade A reflux esophagitis with two small mucosal breaks at the GE junction; no varices, no Barrett's segment. STOMACH: Retroflexion normal. A single 12 mm gastric ulcer was identified in the antrum along the lesser curvature with a non-bleeding visible vessel (Forrest IIa). No active spurting or oozing. Endoscopic hemostasis performed: injection of dilute epinephrine (1:10,000) around the ulcer base followed by placement of two hemostatic clips over the visible vessel; adequate hemostasis achieved. Two biopsies taken from the gastric antrum (away from the ulcer) for H. pylori testing; ulcer margins biopsied to exclude malignancy. DUODENUM: Bulb and D2 normal, no ulcer. IMPRESSION: 12 mm antral gastric ulcer, Forrest IIa (non-bleeding visible vessel), treated with epinephrine injection and hemoclips; LA Grade A esophagitis. Likely NSAID-induced ulcer as source of melena. PLAN: High-dose IV PPI transitioning to oral BID, continue naproxen cessation, H. pylori testing, repeat EGD in 8-12 weeks to confirm ulcer healing given malignancy exclusion biopsies pending.

findings.finding: Gastric ulcer, 12 mm, Forrest IIa (non-bleeding visible vessel)

Normal duodenum to D2

Continue high-dose PPI (pantoprazole 40 mg BID); do NOT resume naproxen — provide alternative analgesia plan for chronic back pain

contacts.verbatim: When will I hear back about the biopsy from my stomach? And can I start taking my naproxen again for my back now?

contacts.expected_action: Reassure that resolution of dark stools is a good sign; explain the repeat EGD in 8-12 weeks is still required to confirm gastric ulcer healing and review pending malignancy-exclusion biopsy. Schedule the surveillance scope.
contacts.rationale: Benign course with an answerable question; links to the repeat_procedure surveillance item. No escalation.

procedure.name: Endoscopic ultrasound with fine-needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) of mediastinal station 7 lymph node
procedure.indication: Diagnosis and staging of subcarinal (station 7) lymphadenopathy with suspicious RUL nodule; tissue sampling to establish/exclude primary lung malignancy
procedure.sedation: Monitored anesthesia care (propofol), anesthesia-directed given mild COPD; well tolerated
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (transesophageal EUS; NPO confirmed, no oral bowel prep required)
procedure.extent_reached: Esophagus and mediastinum; station 7 (subcarinal) node imaged transesophageally with linear echoendoscope

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: EUS-FNA of mediastinal (subcarinal, station 7) lymph node. INDICATION: 2.2 cm spiculated RUL nodule with 1.8 cm station 7 lymphadenopathy suspicious for primary lung malignancy with nodal involvement; no prior tissue diagnosis. ANTICOAGULATION: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (PROPHYLACTIC dosing for post hip-fracture immobility, NOT therapeutic) — morning-of dose held per prophylactic-LMWH periprocedural protocol; no therapeutic bridging applied; INR 1.0, platelets 244. SEDATION: MAC/propofol, anesthesia-directed. FINDINGS: The linear echoendoscope was advanced into the esophagus without difficulty. A rounded, hypoechoic, well-demarcated subcarinal (station 7) lymph node measuring approximately 1.9 x 1.6 cm was identified with features concerning for malignancy (round shape, sharp borders, hypoechoic echotexture). Under real-time Doppler-guided EUS control to avoid intervening vasculature, the node was sampled with a 22-gauge FNA needle for a total of 4 passes; rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) confirmed adequate cellularity with atypical/malignant-appearing cells present. The RUL primary nodule was not directly accessible transesophageally and was not sampled. No immediate procedural bleeding. IMPRESSION: Successful EUS-FNA of station 7 node, adequate sampling with malignant cells on ROSE; final cytology and immunostains pending. Sampling of station 7 (N2 station) has staging implications if malignancy confirmed.

findings.finding: Subcarinal (station 7) lymph node, ~1.9 x 1.6 cm, hypoechoic, round, sharp borders — sonographically suspicious for malignancy
findings.action_taken: EUS-FNA x4 passes, 22G needle, ROSE adequacy confirmed

findings.finding: RUL nodule not accessible transesophageally
findings.action_taken: not sampled; remains radiographically suspicious primary

specimens.type: fine-needle aspiration cytology (4 passes) with cell block for immunohistochemistry

pathology.note: ROSE showed atypical/malignant-appearing cells; final cytology, cell block, and immunohistochemical panel (e.g., TTF-1, p40) pending to confirm primary and lineage; molecular/PD-L1 testing to be requested if non-small cell carcinoma confirmed.

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort after uneventful MAC recovery. No post-procedure chest pain, dyspnea, or fever. Given return precautions for fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, or worsening breathing. Oncology-expedite track continued.

followup.recommendation: Final station 7 cytology/cell block with immunostains pending — ROSE positive for malignant cells; communicate result to patient and forward to oncology/pulmonology to drive staging and treatment
Expedited oncology referral for staging and treatment planning once tissue diagnosis finalized; correlate N2 nodal involvement with PET/staging workup
Resume prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (VTE prophylaxis for hip-fracture immobility) — no therapeutic bridging needed; confirm no post-procedure bleeding before resuming
followup.timeframe: resume same evening / next day per protocol if no bleeding

followup.recommendation: If final cytology is non-diagnostic/benign despite suspicious ROSE and imaging, pursue repeat sampling (repeat EUS-FNA vs EBUS vs CT-guided biopsy of RUL nodule) given high pre-test probability
Correct problem list: 'Malignant tumor of lung' was carried as confirmed without tissue diagnosis; keep as suspected/provisional until final cytology confirms

planted_followup_items.expected_action: track final cytology, call patient with result, route to oncology/pulmonology for staging

contacts.verbatim: My throat's a bit scratchy and sore when I swallow, but I'm eating okay.
contacts.clinical_signal: Expected mild post-transesophageal-EUS sore throat; tolerating oral intake
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; mild sore throat after upper endoscopy/EUS is expected and self-limited over 24-48h; lozenges/warm fluids.
contacts.rationale: Common benign post-endoscopy symptom with preserved swallowing and intake; no escalation.

contacts.verbatim: When will I hear what the sample showed, and when should I start my belly injection again?
contacts.clinical_signal: Patient asking about pending cytology result and resumption of prophylactic enoxaparin
contacts.expected_action: Answer from the result: final cytology expected ~2026-07-28, team will call with result and route to oncology; confirm the daily prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg can resume that evening/next day if no bleeding (no bridging needed).
contacts.rationale: Answerable from the procedure result and follow-up plan; links to pending_pathology (F1) and the med_resume item (F4).

contacts.verbatim: Since yesterday I've had a fever with chills and a deep aching pain in my chest and upper back, and it hurts more when I swallow.
contacts.clinical_signal: Fever/chills with retrosternal/back pain and odynophagia several days after mediastinal EUS-FNA — concern for mediastinitis / esophageal micro-perforation / infected node
contacts.expected_action: Escalate for same-day/ED evaluation: exam, labs, and CT chest to assess for mediastinitis/perforation; do not treat as benign post-procedure soreness; start broad-spectrum antibiotics per surgical/thoracic input if confirmed.
contacts.rationale: Fever plus new chest/back pain and worsening odynophagia is the classic presentation of mediastinitis or esophageal perforation after transesophageal mediastinal FNA — a true, time-critical complication.

contacts.verbatim: The scan area on my chest still feels a little tender when I press on it — is that something to worry about?
contacts.clinical_signal: Localized mild chest-wall tenderness, no fever/dyspnea/systemic signs reported
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; isolated mild tenderness without fever, worsening pain, dyspnea, or swallowing difficulty is expected residual soreness; provide return precautions (fever, worsening chest pain, trouble breathing/swallowing).
contacts.rationale: Benign residual soreness with no systemic red-flag features; safe to reassure with clear return precautions.

procedure.name: Colonoscopy
procedure.indication: Screening colonoscopy — high risk by family history (mother CRC age 48, brother CRC age 52); patient asymptomatic
procedure.sedation: Moderate sedation (midazolam 2 mg, fentanyl 75 mcg)
procedure.prep_quality: Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8/9 — adequate
procedure.extent_reached: Cecum (confirmed by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve); terminal ileum intubated

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Colonoscopy. INDICATION: screening colonoscopy in a 65-year-old man at increased risk due to two first-degree relatives with young-onset colorectal cancer (mother age 48, brother age 52); patient asymptomatic, first lifetime colonoscopy. SEDATION: moderate. PREP: adequate (BBPS 8, all segments >=2). FINDINGS: The colonoscope was advanced to the cecum with anatomic landmarks confirmed; the terminal ileum was intubated and appeared normal. Two diminutive polyps were identified and removed: a 4 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon (cold snare polypectomy, specimen A) and a 3 mm sessile polyp in the transverse colon (cold forceps, specimen B). Both specimens were retrieved and sent to pathology. No mass, ulceration, stricture, or active bleeding was seen. No abnormal mucosa in the rectum. The remainder of the examined colon was normal. IMPRESSION: Two diminutive polyps removed (ascending and transverse colon). No malignancy or suspicious lesion. Pathology pending. Given the strong family history of young-onset colorectal cancer, a shortened surveillance interval and genetic counseling are recommended.

findings.finding: 4 mm sessile polyp
3 mm sessile polyp
Normal terminal ileum, cecum, rectum; no mass, ulcer, stricture, or bleeding

disposition: Discharged home same day in stable condition with escort. Post-polypectomy precautions reviewed. No anticoagulation to resume (patient on no blood thinners).

followup.recommendation: Review polyp pathology (ascending and transverse colon specimens); call patient with results and set surveillance interval accounting for family history
Surveillance colonoscopy interval to be shortened due to two first-degree relatives with young-onset CRC — plan 5-year interval (or sooner per pathology / genetics results)
Complete referral to genetic counseling / germline (Lynch syndrome) evaluation given young-onset CRC in two first-degree relatives

contacts.verbatim: My throat feels a bit scratchy and I've been really gassy and bloated since I got home. Is that normal?
contacts.clinical_signal: Expected post-procedure sore throat and insufflation-related bloating/flatulence after diagnostic colonoscopy
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; mild throat irritation and gas/bloating from air insufflation are expected and self-limited over 24-48h. Walking and warm fluids help. Return precautions for severe or worsening abdominal pain, fever, or bleeding.
contacts.rationale: Classic benign post-endoscopy symptoms; no red flag, no escalation.

contacts.verbatim: The doctor took out a couple of little polyps — when will I find out what they were, and how often do I need to come back given my mom and brother?
contacts.clinical_signal: Patient asking about pending polyp pathology and shortened surveillance interval driven by family history
contacts.expected_action: Answer from the result: two diminutive polyps removed, pathology expected back ~2026-08-09; team will call with results and confirm the shortened surveillance interval (planned ~5 years, adjusted by pathology/genetics). Reinforce the genetics referral offer.
contacts.rationale: Answerable from the procedure result and follow-up plan; maps to the pending_pathology item (F1) and touches surveillance/genetics planning.

contacts.verbatim: I noticed a tiny bit of pink on the toilet paper once yesterday. No real blood, just a little tinge. Should I worry?
contacts.clinical_signal: Trace/minor spotting after diminutive cold-snare and cold-forceps polypectomy in a patient on no anticoagulation — within the benign expected range, no hemodynamic symptoms
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; a single episode of trace pink spotting after diminutive polypectomy (cold technique, no anticoagulant) is expected and self-limited. Give clear return precautions: escalate immediately for repeated or larger volumes of red/maroon blood, clots, dizziness, or abdominal pain.
contacts.rationale: Dangerous-sounding-but-benign: minor spotting after cold polypectomy without anticoagulation is low risk; disposition is reassurance with explicit escalation criteria rather than escalation.
